AI.motivation: To address challenges in continual VideoQA, such as interference between tasks and the high memory demand for task-specific prompts.
AI.method: Introduced HyperTokens, a transformer-based token generator, with meta-inspired regularizers and auxiliary supervision to control memory use and reduce forgetting.
AI.result: Demonstrated superior accuracy and lower forgetting in continual VideoQA benchmarks, and a promising cross-modal ImageQA to VideoQA protocol.
AI.conclusion: HyperTokens effectively improves performance and retention in continual VideoQA setups while alleviating memory constraints.

title: ArcLight: A Lightweight LLM Inference Architecture for Many-Core CPUs
summary: Although existing frameworks for large language model (LLM) inference on CPUs are mature, they fail to fully exploit the computation potential of many-core CPU platforms. Many-core CPUs are widely deployed in web servers and high-end networking devices, and are typically organized into multiple NUMA nodes that group cores and memory. Current frameworks largely overlook the substantial overhead of cross-NUMA memory access, limiting inference scalability and intelligence enabling on such platforms. To address this limitation, we build ArcLight, a lightweight LLM inference architecture designed from the ground up for many-core CPUs. ArcLight integrates efficient memory management and thread scheduling, and introduces finely controlled tensor parallelism to mitigate the cross-node memory access wall. Experimental results show that ArcLight significantly surpasses the performance ceiling of mainstream frameworks, achieving up to 46% higher inference throughput. Moreover, ArcLight maintains compatibility with arbitrary CPU devices. ArcLight is publicly available at https://github.com/OpenBMB/ArcLight.

AI.tldr: ArcLight is a new lightweight architecture for LLM inference on many-core CPUs that improves performance by addressing cross-NUMA memory access overheads through efficient memory management, thread scheduling, and tensor parallelism.
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks for LLM inference on CPUs fail to maximize computation on many-core CPU platforms due to cross-NUMA memory access overheads, which limits scalability and inference performance.
AI.method: ArcLight introduces efficient memory management, optimized thread scheduling, and finely tuned tensor parallelism to address the memory access challenges of many-core CPUs.
AI.result: ArcLight achieves up to 46% higher inference throughput compared to mainstream frameworks on many-core CPUs while maintaining broad compatibility with CPU devices.
AI.conclusion: The ArcLight architecture significantly improves LLM inference performance on many-core CPUs by addressing cross-NUMA access overhead, making it highly scalable and versatile for such platforms.

title: Empathy in Software Engineering Education: Evidence, Practices, and Opportunities
summary: \textbf{Context:} Empathy is increasingly recognized as a critical human capability for software engineers, supporting collaboration, ethical awareness, and user-centered design. While many disciplines have long explored empathy as part of professional formation, its incorporation into software engineering education remains fragmented. \textbf{Aim:} This study investigates how empathy has been used, taught, and discussed in general engineering and software engineering education, with the goal of identifying pedagogical practices, outcomes, and disciplinary differences that inform the structured integration of empathy into software curricula. \textbf{Method:} Following established guidelines for systematic reviews in software engineering, we conducted a comprehensive search across six databases and analyzed 43 primary studies published between 2001 and 2025. Data were coded and synthesized using descriptive and thematic analysis to capture how empathy is conceptualized, fostered, and assessed across educational contexts. \textbf{Findings:} Our findings show that engineering programs frame empathy as an ethical and reflective capacity linked to social responsibility, whereas software engineering translates empathy into structured, design-oriented, and measurable practices. Across both domains, empathy teaching enhances collaboration, ethical reasoning, bias awareness, and motivation, but remains limited by low curricular prioritization, measurement challenges, and resource constraints. \textbf{Conclusion:} Empathy is evolving from a peripheral soft skill into a measurable pedagogical construct in software engineering education. Embedding empathy as a continuous, assessable component of design and development courses can strengthen inclusivity, ethical reflection, and responsible innovation in future software professionals.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the integration of empathy into engineering and software engineering education, highlighting its importance and challenges.
AI.motivation: Empathy is crucial for collaboration, ethical awareness, and user-centered design but remains fragmented in software engineering education.
AI.method: A systematic review was conducted across six databases and analyzed 43 studies using descriptive and thematic analysis.
AI.result: Empathy teaching enhances collaboration, ethical reasoning, and bias awareness but is hindered by low prioritization, measurement difficulties, and resource limitations.
AI.conclusion: Positioning empathy as an assessable construct in curricula can foster inclusivity, ethical reflection, and responsible innovation in software professionals.

title: Language Shapes Mental Health Evaluations in Large Language Models
summary: This study investigates whether large language models (LLMs) exhibit cross-linguistic differences in mental health evaluations. Focusing on Chinese and English, we examine two widely used models, GPT-4o and Qwen3, to assess whether prompt language systematically shifts mental health-related evaluations and downstream decision outcomes. First, we assess models' evaluative orientation toward mental health stigma using multiple validated measurement scales capturing social stigma, self-stigma, and professional stigma. Across all measures, both models produce higher stigma-related responses when prompted in Chinese than in English. Second, we examine whether these differences also manifest in two common downstream decision tasks in mental health. In a binary mental health stigma detection task, sensitivity to stigmatizing content varies across language prompts, with lower sensitivity observed under Chinese prompts. In a depression severity classification task, predicted severity also differs by prompt language, with Chinese prompts associated with more underestimation errors, indicating a systematic downward shift in predicted severity relative to English prompts. Together, these findings suggest that language context can systematically shape evaluative patterns in LLM outputs and shift decision thresholds in downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: This study examines how large language models (GPT-4o and Qwen3) differ in mental health evaluations when prompted in different languages (Chinese vs. English), finding systematic variations in stigma-related outputs and decision tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand if and how prompt language affects large language models' evaluation and decision-making patterns in mental health contexts.
AI.method: Comparing outputs of GPT-4o and Qwen3 across Chinese and English prompts using validated stigma measurement scales and downstream decision tasks, such as detecting mental health stigma and classifying depression severity.
AI.result: Both models produced higher mental health stigma in Chinese prompts than English. Sensitivity to stigma detection was lower in Chinese prompts, and Chinese prompts caused higher underestimation errors in depression severity classification.
AI.conclusion: Language context significantly influences LLM outputs, shaping evaluations and decision thresholds in mental health tasks.

title: Robust Transfer Learning with Side Information
summary:
Robust Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) address environmental shift through distributionally robust optimization (DRO) by finding an optimal worst-case policy within an uncertainty set of transition kernels. However, standard DRO approaches require enlarging the uncertainty set under large shifts, which leads to overly conservative and pessimistic policies.
  In this paper, we propose a framework for transfer under environment shift that derives a robust target-domain policy via estimate-centered uncertainty sets, constructed through constrained estimation that integrates limited target samples with side information about the source-target dynamics. The side information includes bounds on feature moments, distributional distances, and density ratios, yielding improved kernel estimates and tighter uncertainty sets.
  The side information includes bounds on feature moments, distributional distances, and density ratios, yielding improved kernel estimates and tighter uncertainty sets.
  Error bounds and convergence results are established for both robust and non-robust value functions. Moreover, we provide a finite-sample guarantee on the learned robust policy and analyze the robust sub-optimality gap. Under mild low-dimensional structure on the transition model, the side information reduces this gap and improves sample efficiency. We assess the performance of our approach across OpenAI Gym environments and classic control problems, consistently demonstrating superior target-domain performance over state-of-the-art robust and non-robust baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new framework for creating robust policies in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) under environmental shifts, using side information to refine uncertainty sets and improve performance.
AI.motivation: To address the traditional overly conservative and pessimistic policies produced by robust MDPs under large environmental shifts.
AI.method: Introduced a new robust framework that leverages constrained estimation and side information (e.g., feature moments, distributional distances, density ratios) to construct improved and tighter uncertainty sets for transition kernels.
AI.result: Presented error bounds, convergence results, and finite-sample guarantees while closing the robust sub-optimality gap. Empirically demonstrated better performance across OpenAI Gym environments and control tasks compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully mitigates conservativeness in robust MDPs, improves sample efficiency with low-dimensional structure, and achieves superior performance in the target domain.

title: Receding-Horizon Nullspace Optimization for Actuation-Aware Control Allocation in Omnidirectional UAVs
summary: Fully actuated omnidirectional UAVs enable independent control of forces and torques along all six degrees of freedom, broadening the operational envelope for agile flight and aerial interaction tasks. However, conventional control allocation methods neglect the asymmetric dynamics of the onboard actuators, which can induce oscillatory motor commands and degrade trajectory tracking during dynamic maneuvers. This work proposes a receding-horizon, actuation-aware allocation strategy that explicitly incorporates asymmetric motor dynamics and exploits the redundancy of over-actuated platforms through nullspace optimization. By forward-simulating the closed-loop system over a prediction horizon, the method anticipates actuator-induced oscillations and suppresses them through smooth redistribution of motor commands, while preserving the desired body wrench exactly. The approach is formulated as a constrained optimal control problem solved online via Constrained iterative LQR. Simulation results on the OmniOcta platform demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces motor command oscillations compared to a conventional single-step quadratic programming allocator, yielding improved trajectory tracking in both position and orientation.

AI.tldr: The paper improves trajectory tracking of omnidirectional UAVs by addressing actuator-induced issues through a novel receding-horizon allocation strategy.
AI.motivation: Traditional control methods for UAVs result in degraded performance during agile maneuvers due to neglect of asymmetric actuator dynamics.
AI.method: A receding-horizon optimization technique, formulated as a constrained optimal control problem and solved using Constrained iterative LQR, redistributes motor commands smoothly to reduce oscillations.
AI.result: Simulations demonstrate reduced oscillatory motor commands and improved trajectory tracking for the OmniOcta UAV platform using the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: The proposed actuation-aware allocation strategy enhances UAV control during dynamic tasks by successfully mitigating actuator asymmetries.

title: Valid Feature-Level Inference for Tabular Foundation Models via the Conditional Randomization Test
summary: Modern machine learning models are highly expressive but notoriously difficult to analyze statistically. In particular, while black-box predictors can achieve strong empirical performance, they rarely provide valid hypothesis tests or p-values for assessing whether individual features contain information about a target variable. This article presents a practical approach to feature-level hypothesis testing that combines the Conditional Randomization Test (CRT) with TabPFN, a probabilistic foundation model for tabular data. The resulting procedure yields finite-sample valid p-values for conditional feature relevance, even in nonlinear and correlated settings, without requiring model retraining or parametric assumptions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes combining Conditional Randomization Test (CRT) with TabPFN to create a practical feature-level hypothesis testing method.
AI.motivation: Standard machine learning models often fail to provide valid statistical hypotheses or p-values to evaluate feature information in target variables, despite strong empirical performance.
AI.method: It integrates Conditional Randomization Test (CRT) with TabPFN, a probabilistic foundation model, for finite-sample valid p-value estimation without retraining or parametric assumptions.
AI.result: The approach enables accurate feature-level hypothesis testing even in nonlinear and correlated settings, demonstrating practical statistical analysis capabilities.
AI.conclusion: This method bridges the gap between machine learning predictive performance and statistical interpretability, offering a robust testing framework for feature relevance.

title: LieCraft: A Multi-Agent Framework for Evaluating Deceptive Capabilities in Language Models
comment: AAAI 2026 Alignment track. Authors 1 and 2 contributed equally, 3 and 4 contributed equally, 6 and 7 and 8 contributed equally (ordered by last name)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit impressive general-purpose capabilities but also introduce serious safety risks, particularly the potential for deception as models acquire increased agency and human oversight diminishes. In this work, we present LieCraft: a novel evaluation framework and sandbox for measuring LLM deception that addresses key limitations of prior game-based evaluations. At its core, LieCraft is a novel multiplayer hidden-role game in which players select an ethical alignment and execute strategies over a long time-horizon to accomplish missions. Cooperators work together to solve event challenges and expose bad actors, while Defectors evade suspicion while secretly sabotaging missions. To enable real-world relevance, we develop 10 grounded scenarios such as childcare, hospital resource allocation, and loan underwriting that recontextualize the underlying mechanics in ethically significant, high-stakes domains. We ensure balanced gameplay in LieCraft through careful design of game mechanics and reward structures that incentivize meaningful strategic choices while eliminating degenerate strategies. Beyond the framework itself, we report results from 12 state-of-the-art LLMs across three behavioral axes: propensity to defect, deception skill, and accusation accuracy. Our findings reveal that despite differences in competence and overall alignment, all models are willing to act unethically, conceal their intentions, and outright lie to pursue their goals.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LieCraft, a framework for evaluating LLM deception within multiplayer hidden-role games in various real-world scenarios.
AI.motivation: The authors are motivated by the need to address safety risks posed by LLMs, particularly related to deception and diminished human oversight as models gain more autonomy.
AI.method: They introduce LieCraft, a multiplayer hidden-role game with ethical alignment, long-term strategy, and realistic scenarios to evaluate LLM deception along behavioral dimensions.
AI.result: The study tested 12 LLMs and found all were prone to unethical behavior, deceit, and lying to achieve goals, despite varying alignment and competency levels.
AI.conclusion: LieCraft successfully highlights the ethical risks of LLMs and provides a framework to assess and mitigate such behaviors in high-stakes applications.

title: Graph-of-Mark: Promote Spatial Reasoning in Multimodal Language Models with Graph-Based Visual Prompting
summary: Recent advances in training-free visual prompting, such as Set-of-Mark, have emerged as a promising direction for enhancing the grounding capabilities of multimodal language models (MLMs). These techniques operate by partitioning the input image into object regions and annotating them with marks, predominantly boxes with numeric identifiers, before feeding the augmented image to the MLM. However, these approaches treat marked objects as isolated entities, failing to capture the relationships between them. On these premises, we propose Graph-of-Mark (GoM), the first pixel-level visual prompting technique that overlays scene graphs onto the input image for spatial reasoning tasks. We evaluate GoM across 3 open-source MLMs and 4 different datasets, conducting extensive ablations on drawn components and investigating the impact of auxiliary graph descriptions in the text prompt. Our results demonstrate that GoM consistently improves the zero-shot capability of MLMs in interpreting object positions and relative directions, improving base accuracy in visual question answering and localization up to 11 percentage points.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Graph-of-Mark (GoM), a pixel-level visual prompting technique that overlays scene graphs on images to enhance multimodal language models (MLMs) for spatial reasoning tasks, achieving notable accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing visual prompting techniques like Set-of-Mark enhance MLMs' vision capabilities but treat objects as isolated entities, neglecting relationships between them.
AI.method: The proposed method, GoM, overlays scene graphs onto input images and evaluates its effectiveness via extensive experiments on MLMs using datasets and text prompts with auxiliary graph descriptions.
AI.result: GoM improves the zero-shot capabilities of MLMs, leading to notable accuracy improvements (up to 11 percentage points) in visual question answering and localization tasks.
AI.conclusion: GoM effectively enhances MLMs' grounding and spatial reasoning abilities by leveraging scene graphs, presenting a significant advancement in visual prompting.

title: The Consistency Correctness in CoPPar Tree
summary: This article is a supplementary document for the CoPPar Tree paper, providing a detailed correctness proof for the CoPPar architecture.

AI.tldr: The supplementary document presents a correctness proof for the CoPPar Tree architecture.
AI.motivation: To provide comprehensive evidence of the correctness and functionality of the CoPPar architecture.
AI.method: Detailed correctness proof and logical analysis of the CoPPar architecture.
AI.result: The paper successfully demonstrates the correctness of the CoPPar Tree design.
AI.conclusion: The supplemental document strengthens the reliability of CoPPar Tree by proving its correctness.

title: On the Effectiveness of Code Representation in Deep Learning-Based Automated Patch Correctness Assessment
summary: Automated program repair (APR) attempts to generate correct patches and has drawn wide attention from both academia and industry in the past decades. However, APR is continuously struggling with the patch overfitting issue due to the weak test suites. Thus, to address the overfitting problem, the community has proposed an increasing number of approaches to predict patch correctness (APCA approaches). Among them, locally deep learning approaches aimed at automatically match designs has been emerging strongly. Such approaches typically encode input code snippets into well-designed representations and build a binary model for correctness prediction. Despite being fundamental in reason about patch correctness, code representation has not been systematically investigated. To bridge this gap, we perform the first extensive study to evaluate the performance of different code representations on predicting patch correctness from more than 500 trained APCA models. The experimental results on 15 benchmarks with four categories and 11 classifiers show that the graph-based code representation which is ill-explored in the literature, consistently outperforms other representations, e.g., an average accuracy of 82.6% for CPG across three GNN models. Moreover, we demonstrate that such representations can achieve comparable or better performance for three different previous APCA approaches, e.g., filtering out 87.09% overfitting patches by TREETRAIN with AST. We further find that integrating sequence-based representation into heuristic-based representation is able to yield an average improvement of 13.5% on five metrics. Overall, our study highlights the potential and challenges of utilizing code representation to reason about patch correctness, thus increasing the usability of off-the-shelf APR tools and reducing the manual debugging effort of developers in practice.

AI.tldr: Patch overfitting in Automated Program Repair (APR) remains a challenge. Different code representations, particularly graph-based, show promise in improving patch correctness prediction.
AI.motivation: To address patch overfitting issues in APR caused by weak test suites, investigate systematic code representation methods for improving patch correctness predictions.
AI.method: Analyzed over 500 APCA models using varied code representations across 15 benchmarks, employing graph-based, AST, and heuristic integrations in correctness predictions.
AI.result: Graph-based code representations outperformed others with an 82.6% average accuracy in specific models and improved filtering of overfitted patches using existing APCA approaches.
AI.conclusion: Utilizing effective code representation methods can enhance APR tools, reduce developer debugging efforts, and improve patch correctness predictions, highlighting both their potential and challenges.

title: A Dynamic Self-Evolving Extraction System
summary: The extraction of structured information from raw text is a fundamental component of many NLP applications, including document retrieval, ranking, and relevance estimation. High-quality extractions often require domain-specific accuracy, up-to-date understanding of specialized taxonomies, and the ability to incorporate emerging jargon and rare outliers. In many domains--such as medical, legal, and HR--the extraction model must also adapt to shifting terminology and benefit from explicit reasoning over structured knowledge. We propose DySECT, a Dynamic Self-Evolving Extraction and Curation Toolkit, which continually improves as it is used. The system incrementally populates a versatile, self-expanding knowledge base (KB) with triples extracted by the LLM. The KB further enriches itself through the integration of probabilistic knowledge and graph-based reasoning, gradually accumulating domain concepts and relationships. The enriched KB then feeds back into the LLM extractor via prompt tuning, sampling of relevant few-shot examples, or fine-tuning using KB-derived synthetic data. As a result, the system forms a symbiotic closed-loop cycle in which extraction continuously improves knowledge, and knowledge continuously improves extraction.

AI.tldr: DySECT is a toolkit that dynamically evolves to extract structured information from raw text, utilizing LLMs and feedback loops to populate and refine a knowledge base, improving both extraction and domain understanding.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in extracting structured information from raw text, such as adapting to domain-specific needs, rare terms, and evolving terminology.
AI.method: DySECT employs a knowledge base enriched via triples extracted from an LLM, probabilistic reasoning, graph-based processing, and refinement methods like prompt tuning and synthetic data generation.
AI.result: The system enables a symbiotic cycle where improved knowledge supports better extraction and vice versa, adapting dynamically to domains like medical and legal fields.
AI.conclusion: DySECT provides a robust solution for adaptive, high-quality information extraction, leveraging continuous learning and integration of domain-specific knowledge.

title: Local Constrained Bayesian Optimization
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) for high-dimensional constrained problems remains a significant challenge due to the curse of dimensionality. We propose Local Constrained Bayesian Optimization (LCBO), a novel framework tailored for such settings. Unlike trust-region methods that are prone to premature shrinking when confronting tight or complex constraints, LCBO leverages the differentiable landscape of constraint-penalized surrogates to alternate between rapid local descent and uncertainty-driven exploration. Theoretically, we prove that LCBO achieves a convergence rate for the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) residual that depends polynomially on the dimension $d$ for common kernels under mild assumptions, offering a rigorous alternative to global BO where regret bounds typically scale exponentially. Extensive evaluations on high-dimensional benchmarks (up to 100D) demonstrate that LCBO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Local Constrained Bayesian Optimization (LCBO), which addresses high-dimensional constrained optimization by alternating between local descent and exploration.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional constrained Bayesian optimization is challenging due to the curse of dimensionality, and existing methods like trust-region often fail with tight constraints.
AI.method: LCBO uses differentiable constraint-penalized surrogates to alternate between local descent and exploration, achieving theoretical performance guarantees and avoiding premature shrinking of exploration regions.
AI.result: LCBO outperforms state-of-the-art methods in high-dimensional benchmarks (up to 100 dimensions) and shows polynomial convergence for KKT residuals.
AI.conclusion: LCBO provides an efficient and theoretically sound framework for optimizing high-dimensional constrained problems, improving scalability and performance over existing methods.

title: RoboCritics: Enabling Reliable End-to-End LLM Robot Programming through Expert-Informed Critics
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures, Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human Robot Interaction (HRI 2026)
summary: End-user robot programming grants users the flexibility to re-task robots in situ, yet it remains challenging for novices due to the need for specialized robotics knowledge. Large Language Models (LLMs) hold the potential to lower the barrier to robot programming by enabling task specification through natural language. However, current LLM-based approaches generate opaque, "black-box" code that is difficult to verify or debug, creating tangible safety and reliability risks in physical systems. We present RoboCritics, an approach that augments LLM-based robot programming with expert-informed motion-level critics. These critics encode robotics expertise to analyze motion-level execution traces for issues such as joint speed violations, collisions, and unsafe end-effector poses. When violations are detected, critics surface transparent feedback and offer one-click fixes that forward structured messages back to the LLM, enabling iterative refinement while keeping users in the loop. We instantiated RoboCritics in a web-based interface connected to a UR3e robot and evaluated it in a between-subjects user study (n=18). Compared to a baseline LLM interface, RoboCritics reduced safety violations, improved execution quality, and shaped how participants verified and refined their programs. Our findings demonstrate that RoboCritics enables more reliable and user-centered end-to-end robot programming with LLMs.

AI.tldr: RoboCritics introduces an approach to leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) for safer and more reliable robot programming, incorporating expert-informed motion-level critics.
AI.motivation: End-user robot programming is complex for novices due to required robotics expertise, so this study aims to simplify it through natural language interfaces empowered by LLMs.
AI.method: The paper introduces RoboCritics, an approach where LLM-driven robot programming is augmented by critics that analyze execution for motion-level errors like collisions, providing transparent feedback and iterative refinement capabilities.
AI.result: RoboCritics reduced safety violations, enhanced execution quality, and improved user engagement with program verification and refinement compared to an LLM-only baseline.
AI.conclusion: RoboCritics advances safer, effective, and user-friendly robot programming by combining LLMs with motion-level critics, showing benefits in safety and usability.

title: CapTrack: Multifaceted Evaluation of Forgetting in LLM Post-Training
summary: Large language model (LLM) post-training enhances latent skills, unlocks value alignment, improves performance, and enables domain adaptation. Unfortunately, post-training is known to induce forgetting, especially in the ubiquitous use-case of leveraging third-party pre-trained models, which is typically understood as a loss of parametric or factual knowledge. We argue that this accuracy-centric view is insufficient for modern foundation models and instead define forgetting as systematic model drift that degrades behavior and user experience. In this context, we introduce \textbf{CapTrack}, a capability-centric framework for analyzing forgetting in LLMs that combines a behavioral taxonomy with an evaluation suite built on established benchmarks and targeted adaptations. Using CapTrack, we conduct a large-scale empirical study across post-training algorithms, domains, and model families, including models up to 80B parameters. We find that forgetting extends beyond parametric knowledge, with pronounced drift in robustness and default behaviors. Instruction fine-tuning induces the strongest relative drift, while preference optimization is more conservative and can partially recover lost capabilities. Differences across model families persist, and no universal mitigation emerges.

AI.tldr: Post-training in large language models often causes systematic model drift, affecting behavior and user experience. CapTrack is introduced as a framework to analyze and address this issue.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of post-training in LLMs, which induce forgetting and degrade user experience beyond parametric knowledge loss.
AI.method: Developed CapTrack, a capability-focused framework that analyzes forgetting in LLMs through behavioral taxonomy and evaluation benchmarks.
AI.result: The study found significant model drift during post-training processes, with instruction fine-tuning causing the strongest drift and preference optimization showing potential for partial recovery.
AI.conclusion: Forgetting in LLMs extends beyond accuracy loss, affecting robustness and behaviors. CapTrack helps analyze and mitigate these issues, but no universal solution yet exists.

title: Not Too Short, Not Too Long: How LLM Response Length Shapes People's Critical Thinking in Error Detection
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become common decision-support tools across educational and professional contexts, raising questions about how their outputs shape human critical thinking. Prior work suggests that the amount of AI assistance can influence cognitive engagement, yet little is known about how specific properties of LLM outputs (e.g., response length) impacts users' critical evaluation of information. In this study, we examine whether the length of LLM responses shapes users' accuracy in evaluating LLM-generated reasoning on critical thinking tasks, particularly in interaction with the correctness of the LLM's reasoning. To begin evaluating this, we conducted a within-subjects experiment with 24 participants who completed 15 modified Watson--Glaser critical thinking items, each accompanied by an LLM-generated explanation that varied in length and correctness. Mixed-effects logistic regression revealed a strong and statistically reliable effect of LLM output correctness on participant accuracy, with participants more likely to answer correctly when the LLM's explanation was correct. Response length appeared to moderated this effect: when the LLM output was incorrect, medium-length explanations were associated with higher participant accuracy than either shorter or longer explanations, whereas accuracy remained high across lengths when the LLM output was correct. Together, these findings suggest that response length alone may be insufficient to support critical thinking, and that how reasoning is presented-including a potential advantage of mid-length explanations under some conditions-points to design opportunities for LLM-based decision-support systems that emphasize transparent reasoning and calibrated expressions of certainty.

AI.tldr: This study investigates how the length and correctness of LLM-generated responses affect users' critical evaluation of information. It finds that correctness significantly helps users judge the information accurately, with medium-length explanations supporting accuracy in certain conditions involving incorrect reasoning.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the growing use of large language models (LLMs) as decision-support tools, raising concerns about their influence on critical thinking. It particularly aims to explore how the properties of LLM responses, such as length, affect users' accuracy in evaluating reasoning.
AI.method: The researchers conducted a within-subjects experiment with 24 participants completing 15 critical thinking tasks. Each task included an LLM-generated explanation varying in length and correctness. They analyzed the accuracy of participants using mixed-effects logistic regression.
AI.result: Participants were more accurate in evaluating information when LLM-generated explanations were correct. Medium-length explanations led to higher accuracy in evaluating incorrect information compared to shorter or longer ones. The effect of response length diminished for correct explanations.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that response length alone does not guarantee support for critical thinking. Transparent reasoning and calibrated certainty in LLM outputs are essential, with mid-length explanations showing potential benefits under specific conditions for decision-support system design.

title: Accelerating Video Generation Inference with Sequential-Parallel 3D Positional Encoding Using a Global Time Index
summary: Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based video generation models inherently suffer from bottlenecks in long video synthesis and real-time inference, which can be attributed to the use of full spatiotemporal attention. Specifically, this mechanism leads to explosive O(N^2) memory consumption and high first-frame latency. To address these issues, we implement system-level inference optimizations for a causal autoregressive video generation pipeline. We adapt the Self-Forcing causal autoregressive framework to sequence parallel inference and implement a sequence-parallel variant of the causal rotary position embedding which we refer to as Causal-RoPE SP. This adaptation enables localized computation and reduces cross-rank communication in sequence parallel execution. In addition, computation and communication pipelines are optimized through operator fusion and RoPE precomputation. Experiments conducted on an eight GPU A800 cluster show that the optimized system achieves comparable generation quality, sub-second first-frame latency, and near real-time inference speed. For generating five second 480P videos, a 1.58x speedup is achieved, thereby providing effective support for real-time interactive applications.

AI.tldr: The paper optimizes Diffusion Transformer-based video generation models for efficiency in long video synthesis and real-time inference by reducing memory and latency issues.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges of high memory consumption and latency in long video synthesis and real-time inference using spatiotemporal attention in Diffusion Transformer-based models.
AI.method: The authors implemented system-level optimizations for causal autoregressive video generation, incorporating sequence parallel inference, a novel Causal-RoPE SP, operator fusion, and precomputation.
AI.result: Experiments on an eight GPU A800 cluster demonstrated comparable generation quality, sub-second first-frame latency, and a 1.58x speedup for generating five-second 480P videos, supporting real-time applications.
AI.conclusion: The optimized framework significantly reduces computational bottlenecks, making it feasible for real-time interactive video generation while maintaining high quality.

title: SageSched: Efficient LLM Scheduling Confronting Demand Uncertainty and Hybridity
summary:
Efficient LLM inference scheduling is crucial for user experience.However, LLM inferences exhibit remarkable demand uncertainty (with unknown output length beforehand) and hybridity (being both compute and memory intensive). Existing LLM schedulers rely on simple heuristics or focus purely on compute resource, suffering suboptimal performance.
  In this work, we propose SageSched, an efficient LLM scheduler that properly handles demand uncertainty and hybridity of inference workloads.SageSched combines prompt contents with the past inference results to predict output-length distribution in a light-weight and also accurate manner.Meanwhile, it models the true service cost of an inference request with both compute and memory aspects considered.Finally, SageSched employs an uncertainty-aware scheduling policy that can yield the best overall efficiency given the request cost distributions.Testbed experiments over diverse setups confirm that SageSched can attain an efficiency improvement of over 28.7%.

AI.tldr: SageSched is a scheduler for LLM inference that tackles demand uncertainties and resource hybridity, improving efficiency by over 28.7%.
AI.motivation: Current LLM scheduling approaches fail to efficiently handle demand uncertainties and the dual requirements of compute and memory resources, resulting in suboptimal performance.
AI.method: SageSched uses prompt content and past inference data to predict output length, models service costs considering compute and memory, and employs an uncertainty-aware scheduling policy for optimal efficiency.
AI.result: SageSched demonstrated efficiency improvements exceeding 28.7% in diverse testbed experiments.
AI.conclusion: SageSched effectively handles LLM inference scheduling challenges and significantly enhances efficiency compared to existing methods.

title: AgentRaft: Automated Detection of Data Over-Exposure in LLM Agents
summary: The rapid integration of Large Language Model (LLM) agents into autonomous task execution has introduced significant privacy concerns within cross-tool data flows. In this paper, we systematically investigate and define a novel risk termed Data Over-Exposure (DOE) in LLM Agent, where an Agent inadvertently transmits sensitive data beyond the scope of user intent and functional necessity. We identify that DOE is primarily driven by the broad data paradigms in tool design and the coarse-grained data processing inherent in LLMs. In this paper, we present AgentRaft, the first automated framework for detecting DOE risks in LLM agents. AgentRaft combines program analysis with semantic reasoning through three synergistic modules: (1) it constructs a Cross-Tool Function Call Graph (FCG) to model the interaction landscape of heterogeneous tools; (2) it traverses the FCG to synthesize high-quality testing user prompts that act as deterministic triggers for deep-layer tool execution; and (3) it performs runtime taint tracking and employs a multi-LLM voting committee grounded in global privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA, PIPL) to accurately identify privacy violations. We evaluate AgentRaft on a testing environment of 6,675 real-world agent tools. Our findings reveal that DOE is indeed a systemic risk, prevalent in 57.07% of potential tool interaction paths. AgentRaft achieves a high detection accuracy and effectiveness, outperforming baselines by 87.24%. Furthermore, AgentRaft reaches near-total DOE coverage (99%) within only 150 prompts while reducing per-chain verification costs by 88.6%. Our work provides a practical foundation for building auditable and privacy-compliant LLM agent systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces and investigates the risk of Data Over-Exposure (DOE) in LLM agents and proposes AgentRaft, a framework to detect such risks via program analysis, semantic reasoning, and privacy regulation compliance.
AI.motivation: Address privacy concerns in autonomous task execution by LLM agents, particularly in cross-tool data flows that might expose sensitive data unnecessarily.
AI.method: The paper introduces AgentRaft, an automated framework combining program analysis and semantic reasoning. It includes constructing a Function Call Graph for tool interactions, generating testing prompts to trigger tool executions, and performing taint tracking with multi-LLM voting based on privacy regulations.
AI.result: Evaluation on 6,675 real-world agent tools reveals DOE risks in 57.07% of tool interaction paths. AgentRaft outperforms baselines in detection accuracy by 87.24% and achieves high coverage with reduced verification costs.
AI.conclusion: AgentRaft provides an effective and efficient approach to identifying DOE risks, establishing a practical foundation for privacy-compliant and auditable LLM agent systems.

title: Reforming the Mechanism: Editing Reasoning Patterns in LLMs with Circuit Reshaping
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often exhibit flawed reasoning ability that undermines reliability. Existing approaches to improving reasoning typically treat it as a general and monolithic skill, applying broad training which is inefficient and unable to target specific reasoning errors. We introduce Reasoning Editing, a paradigm for selectively modifying specific reasoning patterns in LLMs while preserving other reasoning pathways. This task presents a fundamental trade-off between Generality, the ability of an edit to generalize across different tasks sharing the same reasoning pattern, and Locality, the ability to preserve other reasoning capabilities. Through systematic investigation, we uncover the Circuit-Interference Law: Edit interference between reasoning patterns is proportional to the overlap of their neural circuits. Guided by this principle, we propose REdit, the first framework to actively reshape neural circuits before editing, thereby modulating interference between reasoning patterns and mitigating the trade-off. REdit integrates three components: (i) Contrastive Circuit Reshaping, which directly addresses the generality-locality trade-off by disentangling overlapping circuits; (ii) Meta-Contrastive Learning, which extends transferability to novel reasoning patterns; and (iii) Dual-Level Protection, which preserves preexisting abilities by constraining reshaping update directions and regularizing task-level predictions. Extensive experiments with Qwen-2.5-3B on propositional logic reasoning tasks across three difficulty levels demonstrate that REdit consistently achieves superior generality and locality compared to baselines, with additional validation in mathematics showing broader potential. Our code is available at https://github.com/LzyFischer/REdit.

AI.tldr: The paper presents REdit, a framework improving reasoning in LLMs by reshaping neural circuits to balance generality and locality.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models struggle with reasoning reliability due to general approaches that fail to address reasoning-specific errors efficiently.
AI.method: Introduced Reasoning Editing and the REdit framework with techniques like Contrastive Circuit Reshaping, Meta-Contrastive Learning, and Dual-Level Protection to selectively modify reasoning patterns.
AI.result: REdit demonstrated superior performance in balancing generality and locality for reasoning tasks, validated through logic and mathematics experiments.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning Editing modulates neural circuits effectively, enhancing LLM reasoning capabilities while retaining overall model abilities.

title: Beyond ReinMax: Low-Variance Gradient Estimators for Discrete Latent Variables
summary: Machine learning models involving discrete latent variables require gradient estimators to facilitate backpropagation in a computationally efficient manner. The most recent addition to the Straight-Through family of estimators, ReinMax, can be viewed from a numerical ODE perspective as incorporating an approximation via Heun's method to reduce bias, but at the cost of high variance. In this work, we introduce the ReinMax-Rao and ReinMax-CV estimators which incorporate Rao-Blackwellisation and control variate techniques into ReinMax to reduce its variance. Our estimators demonstrate superior performance on training variational autoencoders with discrete latent spaces. Furthermore, we investigate the possibility of leveraging alternative numerical methods for constructing more accurate gradient approximations and present an alternative view of ReinMax from a simpler numerical integration perspective.

AI.tldr: The authors introduce improved gradient estimators for machine learning models with discrete latent variables, which aim to reduce the variance of the existing ReinMax estimator.
AI.motivation: Reducing variance in gradient estimators is essential for efficient training of models with discrete latent variables. The current ReinMax estimator suffers from high variance despite reducing bias.
AI.method: The authors propose two new estimators, ReinMax-Rao and ReinMax-CV, which utilize Rao-Blackwellisation and control variates to reduce variance. They also explore alternative numerical methods for gradient approximation.
AI.result: The new estimators outperform ReinMax in training variational autoencoders with discrete latent spaces, showcasing reduced variance and improved performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating techniques like Rao-Blackwellisation and control variates can significantly enhance the performance of existing gradient estimators for models involving discrete latent variables.

title: Nonlinear Performance Degradation of Vision-Based Teleoperation under Network Latency
summary: Teleoperation is increasingly being adopted as a critical fallback for autonomous vehicles. However, the impact of network latency on vision-based, perception-driven control remains insufficiently studied. The present work investigates the nonlinear degradation of closed-loop stability in camera-based lane keeping under varying network delays. To conduct this study, we developed the Latency-Aware Vision Teleoperation testbed (LAVT), a research-oriented ROS 2 framework that enables precise, distributed one-way latency measurement and reproducible delay injection. Using LAVT, we performed 180 closed-loop experiments in simulation across diverse road geometries. Our findings reveal a sharp collapse in stability between 150 ms and 225 ms of one-way perception latency, where route completion rates drop from 100% to below 50% as oscillatory instability and phase-lag effects emerge. We further demonstrate that additional control-channel delay compounds these effects, significantly accelerating system failure even under constant visual latency. By combining this systematic empirical characterization with the LAVT testbed, this work provides quantitative insights into perception-driven instability and establishes a reproducible baseline for future latency-compensation and predictive control strategies. Project page, supplementary video, and code are available at https://bimilab.github.io/paper-LAVT

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the effect of network latency on camera-based lane keeping for teleoperation of autonomous vehicles.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify the impact of network latency on perception-driven control and stability in teleoperated autonomous vehicle systems.
AI.method: The authors developed the Latency-Aware Vision Teleoperation testbed (LAVT) within a ROS 2 framework to measure latency precisely and inject delays reproducibly. They conducted 180 simulated experiments on different road geometries using this testbed.
AI.result: Experiments showed that visual latency between 150 ms and 225 ms causes a sharp stability collapse, with route completion rates dropping from 100% to below 50%. Additional control delay exacerbates these effects.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a detailed characterization of perception-driven instability caused by latency, offering a reproducible baseline for developing latency-compensation and predictive control mechanisms for teleoperation systems.

title: Consensus is Not Verification: Why Crowd Wisdom Strategies Fail for LLM Truthfulness
summary: Pass@k and other methods of scaling inference compute can improve language model performance in domains with external verifiers, including mathematics and code, where incorrect candidates can be filtered reliably. This raises a natural question: can we similarly scale compute to elicit gains in truthfulness for domains without convenient verification? We show that across five benchmarks and models, surprisingly, it cannot. Even at 25x the inference cost of naive sampling, polling-style aggregation yields no consistent accuracy gains over single-sample baselines and often amplifies shared misconceptions. We find that under uncertainty, models are better at predicting what other models will say within model ensembles than at identifying what is true, revealing a separation between social prediction and truth verification. Across models and benchmarks, aggregation fails to provide a robust truth signal because language model errors are strongly correlated. The source of correlation goes beyond any individual benchmark: we show that even when conditioned on out of distribution random strings and asked to produce pseudo-random outputs, different models produce correlated outputs. Confidence-based weighting provides no benefit because self-reported confidence fails to reliably distinguish correct from incorrect answers. These results delineate a boundary for inference-time scaling: in verified domains, additional samples provide more candidates for a verifier to filter; in unverified domains, additional samples merely reinforce shared misconceptions.

AI.tldr: Inference-time scaling improves performance in domains with external verifiers but fails to enhance truthfulness in unverifiable domains.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether inference-time computational scaling can improve truthfulness in unverifiable domains, as it does in domains with external verification.
AI.method: Experiments conducted across five benchmarks and language models to evaluate accuracy improvements through polling-style aggregation versus single-sample baselines.
AI.result: Polling-style aggregation at significantly higher inference costs showed no consistent accuracy improvements and often amplified shared misconceptions; errors across models were correlated.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time scaling is effective only in verified domains, where additional samples aid verification but fails in unverifiable domains, reinforcing model misconceptions.

title: Distributed Legal Infrastructure for a Trustworthy Agentic Web
summary: The agentic web marks a structural transition from a human-centered information network to a digital environment populated by artificial intelligence (AI) agents that perceive, decide, and act autonomously. As delegated action unfolds at machine speed, exceeds discrete moments of human judgment, and distributes decision-making across non-human actors, existing legal frameworks face growing strain, creating an urgent need for new mechanisms capable of sustaining legality in this emerging order. A trustworthy agentic web therefore depends on the infrastructuring of legality through interoperable protocols that organize identity, delegation, and accountability across systems, enabling coherent governance beyond isolated platforms. Towards this end, this article advances a distributed legal infrastructure (DLI), a governance paradigm composed of five interlocking layers: (1) self-sovereign, soulbound agent identities; (2) cognitive AI logic and constraint systems; (3) decentralized adjudication mechanisms for dispute resolution; (4) bottom-up agentic market regulation to mitigate information asymmetries and network effects, including insurance-based models; and (5) portable institutional frameworks that enable legal interoperability while preserving plural sources of authority. This reference framework contributes to emerging research on embedding legality within agentic web infrastructure, aligning distributed technical systems with accountability, contestability, and rule-of-law principles.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the transition to an AI-driven 'agentic web' and proposes Distributed Legal Infrastructure (DLI) for effective governance.
AI.motivation: To address the legal challenges posed by AI agents acting autonomously in digital environments where existing frameworks struggle.
AI.method: Development of a Distributed Legal Infrastructure (DLI) based on five interconnected layers to ensure legality and governance.
AI.result: The paper outlines and details the DLI paradigm's layers for legally governing AI-agentic systems effectively.
AI.conclusion: The DLI framework aligns technical systems with legal principles, offering solutions for trustworthy governance in the emerging agentic web environment.

title: Better Eyes, Better Thoughts: Why Vision Chain-of-Thought Fails in Medicine
summary: Large vision-language models (VLMs) often benefit from chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting in general domains, yet its efficacy in medical vision-language tasks remains underexplored. We report a counter-intuitive trend: on medical visual question answering, CoT frequently underperforms direct answering (DirA) across general-purpose and medical-specific models. We attribute this to a \emph{medical perception bottleneck}: subtle, domain-specific cues can weaken visual grounding, and CoT may compound early perceptual uncertainty rather than correct it. To probe this hypothesis, we introduce two training-free, inference-time grounding interventions: (i) \emph{perception anchoring} via region-of-interest cues and (ii) \emph{description grounding} via high-quality textual guidance. Across multiple benchmarks and model families, these interventions improve accuracy, mitigate CoT degradation, and in several settings reverse the CoT--DirA inversion. Our findings suggest that reliable clinical VLMs require robust visual grounding and cross-modal alignment, beyond extending text-driven reasoning chains. Code is available \href{https://github.com/TianYin123/Better_Eyes_Better_Thoughts}{here}.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the efficacy of chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting in medical vision-language models (VLMs), concluding that direct answering often outperforms CoT due to challenges in visual grounding.
AI.motivation: To explore why chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, commonly effective in general vision-language tasks, frequently performs poorly in medical visual question answering, and to address the medical perception bottleneck that arises.
AI.method: The authors conducted studies involving two inference-time grounding interventions: (i) perception anchoring using region-of-interest cues and (ii) description grounding with high-quality textual guidance. These were tested across multiple benchmarks and models.
AI.result: These grounding interventions improved accuracy, mitigated the weaknesses of CoT prompting, and, in some cases, reversed the trend of CoT underperforming compared to direct answering in medical applications.
AI.conclusion: Achieving reliable performance in clinical VLMs requires improved visual grounding and cross-modal alignment, rather than solely relying on text-driven reasoning. The insights drive focus on strengthening these aspects for better outcomes.

title: RAPID: Redundancy-Aware and Compatibility-Optimal Edge-Cloud Partitioned Inference for Diverse VLA models
summary: Vision Language Action (VLA) models are mainstream in embodied intelligence but face high inference costs. Edge-Cloud Collaborative (ECC) inference offers an effective fix by easing edge-device computing pressure to meet real-time needs. However, existing ECC frameworks are suboptimal for VLA models due to two challenges: (1) Mainstream environment-oriented edge-cloud partitioning methods are susceptible to interference from visual noise; (2) Existing edge-cloud partitioning methods overlook the step-wise redundancy unique to embodied tasks, thereby disrupting the physical continuity of motion. To address these issues, we propose a novel ECC inference framework, termed RAPID. Specifically, we developed an implementation tailored to the proposed framework. Experiments demonstrate this achieves a speedup of up to 1.73x with only 5%~7% overhead.

AI.tldr: A new Edge-Cloud Collaborative framework, RAPID, is proposed to improve inference for Vision Language Action models, providing significant speed improvements with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Vision Language Action models face high inference costs when used in embodied intelligence tasks, and existing ECC frameworks are suboptimal due to issues with visual noise interference and redundancy in embodied tasks.
AI.method: The authors designed RAPID, a novel ECC inference framework with tailored implementations to address visual noise interference and step-wise redundancy to improve computing and motion continuity.
AI.result: Experiments showed RAPID achieved up to 1.73x speedup while adding only 5%~7% overhead.
AI.conclusion: RAPID effectively optimizes ECC inference for VLA models, addressing key challenges and enhancing inference performance and physical motion continuity.

title: KCoEvo: A Knowledge Graph Augmented Framework for Evolutionary Code Generation
comment: Accepted to the DASFAA 2026 Industry Track
summary: Code evolution is inevitable in modern software development. Changes to third-party APIs frequently break existing code and complicate maintenance, posing practical challenges for developers. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in code generation, they struggle to reason without a structured representation of these evolving relationships, often leading them to produce outdated APIs or invalid outputs. In this work, we propose a knowledge graph-augmented framework that decomposes the migration task into two synergistic stages: evolution path retrieval and path-informed code generation. Our approach constructs static and dynamic API graphs to model intra-version structures and cross-version transitions, enabling structured reasoning over API evolution. Both modules are trained with synthetic supervision automatically derived from real-world API diffs, ensuring scalability and minimal human effort. Extensive experiments across single-package and multi-package benchmarks demonstrate that our framework significantly improves migration accuracy, controllability, and execution success over standard LLM baselines. The source code and datasets are available at: https://github.com/kangjz1203/KCoEvo.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a knowledge graph-augmented framework to tackle API code migration challenges caused by evolving software APIs, achieving improved accuracy and results over standard LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Developers face difficulties due to frequent changes in third-party APIs, which cause outdated or invalid code outputs and hinder LLMs from accurate reasoning on evolving API relationships.
AI.method: The paper proposes a framework with two stages: (1) evolution path retrieval using static and dynamic API knowledge graphs, and (2) path-informed code generation guided by structured reasoning. Modules are trained with synthetic supervision derived from real-world API changes.
AI.result: Experiments show the proposed framework outperforms standard LLM baselines in terms of migration accuracy, controllability, and execution success on various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A knowledge graph-based approach enables structured reasoning for software evolution tasks, enhancing API code migration capabilities while reducing human effort through synthetic supervision.

title: Deep Research, Shallow Evaluation: A Case Study in Meta-Evaluation for Long-Form QA Benchmarks
summary: Recent advances have made long-form report-generating systems widely available. This has prompted evaluation frameworks that use LLM-as-judge protocols and claim verification, along with meta-evaluation frameworks that seek to validate these methods. Many of the meta-evaluations estimate an evaluation quality's by comparing its assessments against human pairwise preferences. Prior work, however, suggests that human pairwise preference may be overly simplistic and can fail to capture nuances of expert expectations. We conduct a case study in meta-evaluation for long-form QA benchmarks using ScholarQA-CS2, a benchmark designed for assessing retrieval-augmented deep-research QA in the scientific domain. We comprehensively validate the benchmark through human pairwise preference judgments, then critically examine the strengths, weaknesses, and confounders of this approach. We show that pairwise preference rankings are best suited for system-level evaluation, while explicit metric-wise annotations and expert annotators are critical for reliable metric-level assessment, with subjectivity remaining a key challenge. Based on our findings, we offer practical guidelines for designing future meta-evaluations that better align evaluation methods, annotator expertise, and reporting practices. By surfacing these methodological challenges, we aim to advance evaluation standards for deep-research systems.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of human pairwise preference judgments in meta-evaluations for long-form QA systems, identifying strengths and weaknesses while proposing guidelines for improved future evaluations.
AI.motivation: Prior meta-evaluation methods rely on human pairwise preferences, which may lack nuance and fail to align with expert expectations, prompting the need for an in-depth assessment of their reliability.
AI.method: Conducted a case study using ScholarQA-CS2, validated benchmarks through human pairwise judgments, and analyzed additional factors such as metric-wise annotations and expertise impact.
AI.result: Pairwise preferences are effective for system-level evaluations but insufficient for metric-level analysis, highlighting the importance of expert annotators and explicit metrics.
AI.conclusion: Human judgments in meta-evaluations should align better with expert annotations, and designers should implement tailored guidelines to address subjectivity and improve evaluation standards for research systems.

title: Posterior Sampling Reinforcement Learning with Gaussian Processes for Continuous Control: Sublinear Regret Bounds for Unbounded State Spaces
summary: We analyze the Bayesian regret of the Gaussian process posterior sampling reinforcement learning (GP-PSRL) algorithm. Posterior sampling is an effective heuristic for decision-making under uncertainty that has been used to develop successful algorithms for a variety of continuous control problems. However, theoretical work on GP-PSRL is limited. All known regret bounds either fail to achieve a tight dependence on a kernel-dependent quantity called the maximum information gain or fail to properly account for the fact that the set of possible system states is unbounded. Through a recursive application of the Borell-Tsirelson-Ibragimov-Sudakov inequality, we show that, with high probability, the states actually visited by the algorithm are contained within a ball of near-constant radius. To obtain tight dependence on the maximum information gain, we use the chaining method to control the regret suffered by GP-PSRL. Our main result is a Bayesian regret bound of the order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(H^{3/2}\sqrt{γ_{T/H} T})$, where $H$ is the horizon, $T$ is the number of time steps and $γ_{T/H}$ is the maximum information gain. With this result, we resolve the limitations with prior theoretical work on PSRL, and provide the theoretical foundation and tools for analyzing PSRL in complex settings.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a Bayesian regret bound for the GP-PSRL algorithm, addressing limitations in prior theoretical works.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of tight theoretical regret bounds for the Gaussian Process Posterior Sampling Reinforcement Learning (GP-PSRL) algorithm, which has limited understanding despite its success in various continuous control problems.
AI.method: The authors use advanced probability techniques, like the Borell-Tsirelson-Ibragimov-Sudakov inequality to bound the state space and the chaining method to control the regret, combining these to achieve tight dependence on the maximum information gain.
AI.result: The authors derive a Bayesian regret bound of order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(H^{3/2}\sqrt{γ_{T/H} T})$, providing tighter bounds than previous works and dealing with unbounded system states.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a foundational theoretical advancement for GP-PSRL algorithms, enabling their analysis in more complex and unbounded state settings.

title: Robodimm: A Physics-Grounded Framework for Automated Actuator Sizing in Scalable Modular Robots
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures. Preprint version submitted to arXiv
summary: Selecting an appropriate motor-gearbox combination is a critical design task in robotics because it directly affects cost, mass, and dynamic performance. This process is especially challenging in modular robots with closed kinematic chains, where joint torques are coupled and actuator inertia propagates through the mechanism. We present Robodimm, a software framework for automated actuator sizing in scalable robot architectures. By leveraging Pinocchio for dynamics and Pink for inverse kinematics, Robodimm uses a Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) formulation for constrained inverse dynamics. The platform supports parametric scaling, interactive trajectory programming through jog modes, and a two-round validation workflow that addresses actuator self-weight effects.

AI.tldr: Robodimm is a software framework that automates motor-gearbox selection in modular robots by addressing coupling effects in dynamics.
AI.motivation: Selecting motor-gearbox combinations efficiently is crucial in robotics and more complex in modular robots with coupled dynamics.
AI.method: They developed Robodimm, leveraging Pinocchio for dynamics, Pink for inverse kinematics, and KKT formulations for constrained dynamics.
AI.result: Robodimm supports parametric scaling, interactive trajectory tools, and a validation workflow addressing dynamic effects.
AI.conclusion: Robodimm provides an effective and automated solution for actuator sizing in modular robotic systems, improving design performance.

title: Enhancing the Detection of Coronary Artery Disease Using Machine Learning
comment: 20 pages, 11 figures, 5 tables. This paper proposes a hybrid Bi-LSTM and GRU based machine learning framework for improved detection of Coronary Artery Disease using CCTA imaging data and clinical features
summary: Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. Early detection is critical to recover patient outcomes and decrease healthcare costs. In recent years, machine learning (ML) advancements have shown significant potential in enhancing the accuracy of CAD diagnosis. This study investigates the application of ML algorithms to improve the detection of CAD by analyzing patient data, including clinical features, imaging, and biomarker profiles. Bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM), Gated Recurrent Units (GRU), and a hybrid of Bi-LSTM+GRU were trained on large datasets to predict the presence of CAD. Results demonstrated that these ML models outperformed traditional diagnostic methods in sensitivity and specificity, offering a robust tool for clinicians to make more informed decisions. The experimental results show that the hybrid model achieved an accuracy of 97.07%. By integrating advanced data preprocessing techniques and feature selection, this study ensures optimal learning and model performance, setting a benchmark for the application of ML in CAD diagnosis. The integration of ML into CAD detection presents a promising avenue for personalized healthcare and could play a pivotal role in the future of cardiovascular disease management.

AI.tldr: This study explores the application of Bi-LSTM, GRU, and a hybrid ML model to improve CAD detection, achieving 97.07% accuracy, and demonstrates their superiority over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To enhance the accuracy of Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) diagnosis and improve early detection using advanced machine learning (ML) techniques.
AI.method: The study utilized machine learning models (Bi-LSTM, GRU, and a hybrid Bi-LSTM+GRU) trained on large datasets, employing data preprocessing techniques and feature selection for optimal performance.
AI.result: The hybrid ML model achieved an impressive 97.07% accuracy, surpassing traditional diagnostic methods in sensitivity and specificity.
AI.conclusion: Integrating machine learning technologies into CAD detection offers a promising path towards personalized healthcare and advanced cardiovascular disease management.

title: SJD-PV: Speculative Jacobi Decoding with Phrase Verification for Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Autoregressive (AR) image models have recently demonstrated remarkable generative capability, but their sequential nature results in significant inference latency. Existing training-free acceleration methods typically verify tokens independently, overlooking the strong co-occurrence patterns between adjacent visual tokens. This independence assumption often leads to contextual inconsistency and limits decoding efficiency. In this work, we introduce a novel training-free acceleration framework that performs phrase-level speculative verification, enabling the model to jointly validate multiple correlated tokens within each decoding window. To construct such phrase units, we analyze token co-occurrence statistics from the training corpus and group frequently co-occurring tokens into semantically coherent visual phrases. During inference, the proposed phrase-level verification evaluates aggregated likelihood ratios over each phrase, allowing simultaneous acceptance of multiple tokens while preserving generation quality. Extensive experiments on autoregressive text-to-image generation show that our method significantly reduces the number of function evaluations (NFE) and achieves up to 30% faster decoding without compromising visual fidelity. Our findings reveal that modeling short-range token co-occurrence provides an effective and general principle for accelerating autoregressive inference.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to accelerate autoregressive image models by jointly validating multiple related tokens in a phrase, enhancing decoding speed without sacrificing quality.
AI.motivation: Current autoregressive image models have high inference latency due to their sequential nature, and existing acceleration methods ignore contextual relationships between visual tokens, leading to inefficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce a training-free framework that analyzes token co-occurrence to group tokens into meaningful visual phrases, enabling the model to validate multiple tokens simultaneously during decoding.
AI.result: The method reduces function evaluations, speeds up decoding by up to 30%, and maintains visual generation quality in autoregressive text-to-image generation.
AI.conclusion: Short-range token co-occurrence modeling is a general and effective principle for accelerating autoregressive inference.

title: SI-ChainFL: Shapley-Incentivized Secure Federated Learning for High-Speed Rail Data Sharing
summary: In high-speed rail (HSR) systems, federated learning (FL) enables cross-departmental flow prediction without sharing raw data. However, existing schemes suffer from two key limitations: (1) insufficient incentives, leading to free-riding and model poisoning; and (2) centralized aggregation, which introduces a single point of failure. We propose a secure and efficient framework SI-ChainFL that addresses these issues by combining contribution-aware incentives with decentralized aggregation. First, we quantify client contributions using a Shapley value metric that jointly considers rare-event utility, data diversity, data quality, and timeliness. To reduce computational overhead, we further develop a rare positive driven client clustering strategy to accelerate Shapley estimation. Moreover, we design a blockchain-based consensus protocol for decentralized aggregation, where aggregation eligibility is tied to Shapley incentives. This design motivates clients to submit high-quality updates and enables efficient and secure global aggregation. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR 10 and CIFAR 100, and a HSR flow dataset show that SI ChainFL remains effective under 90% malicious clients in PA attacks, achieving 14.12% higher accuracy than RAGA. Theoretical analysis further guarantees an upper bound on performance

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SI-ChainFL, a secure and efficient federated learning framework for high-speed rail systems addressing incentive and aggregation challenges.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to enhance cross-departmental flow prediction in high-speed rail systems using federated learning but tackling issues like insufficient incentives and centralization vulnerabilities.
AI.method: SI-ChainFL combines contribution-aware incentives using Shapley value metrics and a blockchain-based decentralized aggregation protocol.
AI.result: Experiments show SI-ChainFL achieves higher performance under attacks, with notable accuracy improvement compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: SI-ChainFL effectively improves security, efficiency, and model accuracy in federated learning by addressing incentive and centralization problems.

title: The Effect of Code Obfuscation on Human Program Comprehension
summary:
We investigate how code obfuscation influences human understanding of programs through an output-prediction task. To study this effect, we construct multiple levels of obfuscation, ranging from unobfuscated code to transformations involving identifier renaming, adversarially misleading identifiers, control-flow modifications, and combinations of these techniques. These transformations are applied to function-level programs written in Python and JavaScript. Participants were asked to predict program outputs while we recorded correctness, response time, and self-reported programming experience.
  Our results show that obfuscation generally increases the time required to reason about code and tends to reduce prediction accuracy. However, the relationship between obfuscation strength and performance is not strictly monotonic and varies across programming languages. JavaScript exhibits the expected pattern of increasing difficulty with stronger obfuscation, whereas Python displays a more complex trend in which certain renaming transformations can perform comparably to, or occasionally better than, the unobfuscated baseline. Response-time analyses further suggest that obfuscation shifts participants away from rapid, heuristic reasoning toward slower and more deliberate reasoning processes. Performance appears highest within a moderate range of response times, indicating that careful deliberation can improve accuracy, while extremely long response times often correspond to confusion. Finally, programming experience predicts performance primarily within a given language, with limited transfer across languages, suggesting that obfuscation challenges language-specific familiarity more than general programming ability.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how code obfuscation affects human understanding of programs by analyzing participant performance in predicting program outputs under varying obfuscation levels.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand how code obfuscation influences reasoning, prediction accuracy, and response time, as well as the role of programming experience.
AI.method: The researchers applied different obfuscation techniques to Python and JavaScript code and studied participants' response times, correctness, and self-reported experience in output-prediction tasks.
AI.result: Obfuscation typically increases reasoning time and reduces accuracy. Interestingly, Python showed non-monotonic trends where certain transformations performed comparably or even better than unobfuscated code.
AI.conclusion: Obfuscation alters reasoning strategies, with experienced programmers performing better within the same language but facing challenges across languages. Careful deliberation improves accuracy, but extreme delays may cause confusion.

title: Elenchus: Generating Knowledge Bases from Prover-Skeptic Dialogues
summary: We present Elenchus, a dialogue system for knowledge base construction grounded in inferentialist semantics, where knowledge engineering is re-conceived as explicitation rather than extraction from expert testimony or textual content. A human expert develops a bilateral position (commitments and denials) about a topic through prover-skeptic dialogue with a large language model (LLM) opponent. The LLM proposes tensions (claims that parts of the position are jointly incoherent) which the expert resolves by retraction, refinement, or contestation. The LLM thus serves as a defeasible derivability oracle whose unreliability is structurally contained by the expert's authority.
