Our main technical contribution is a mapping from Elenchus dialectical states to material bases in Hlobil and Brandom's NonMonotonic MultiSuccedent (NMMS) logic, satisfying Containment and enabling the elaboration of logical vocabulary that makes explicit the inferential relationships negotiated in the dialectic. We demonstrate the approach on the W3C PROV-O provenance ontology, where a single dialogue session elicits and structures design tensions that a domain expert can articulate, corresponding to decisions documented in a retrospective analysis of the ontology's design. Using pyNMMS, an automated NMMS reasoner, we verify that the structural properties of the resulting material base (nontransitivity, nonmonotonicity, and independence) correspond to specific PROV design rationales, demonstrating end-to-end integration from dialogue through formal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Elenchus is a dialogue-based system for knowledge base construction, rethinking knowledge engineering as explicitation instead of knowledge extraction. It uses a structured dialogue with a large language model to resolve logical inconsistencies and refine expert knowledge.
AI.motivation: To provide a novel methodology for knowledge base construction that emphasizes explicitation over extraction, grounding in inferentialist semantics to engage expert knowledge effectively.
AI.method: A human expert interacts in a prover-skeptic dialogue with a language model, resolving tensions and inconsistencies identified by the model. This interaction maps to NonMonotonic MultiSuccedent logic for inferentially structured reasoning.
AI.result: The approach was demonstrated using the W3C PROV-O ontology. It successfully elicited and structured design issues, which were verified via automated reasoning, showing alignment with documented design rationales.
AI.conclusion: Elenchus illustrates how dialogue-driven explicitation complemented with formal reasoning can effectively support knowledge base construction, making inferential relationships explicit while containing LLM unreliability.

title: Unifying On- and Off-Policy Variance Reduction Methods
summary:
Continuous and efficient experimentation is key to the practical success of user-facing applications on the web, both through online A/B-tests and off-policy evaluation. Despite their shared objective -- estimating the incremental value of a treatment -- these domains often operate in isolation, utilising distinct terminologies and statistical toolkits. This paper bridges that divide by establishing a formal equivalence between their canonical variance reduction methods.
  We prove that the standard online Difference-in-Means estimator is mathematically identical to an off-policy Inverse Propensity Scoring estimator equipped with an optimal (variance-minimising) additive control variate. Extending this unification, we demonstrate that widespread regression adjustment methods (such as CUPED, CUPAC, and ML-RATE) are structurally equivalent to Doubly Robust estimation. This unified view extends our understanding of commonly used approaches, and can guide practitioners and researchers working on either class of problems.

AI.tldr: The paper bridges the gap between online A/B tests and off-policy evaluation by establishing a formal equivalence in variance reduction methods, providing a unified view.
AI.motivation: To address the disconnect and isolation between experimentation methods used in online A/B-tests and off-policy evaluation, despite their shared goal of estimating treatment effects.
AI.method: The authors prove mathematical equivalence between online Difference-in-Means and off-policy Inverse Propensity Scoring methods with optimal control variates, and extend it by equivalence to Doubly Robust estimation with regression adjustment methods.
AI.result: The paper establishes structural equivalences between key statistical methods used in online and off-policy experiments, offering a unified theoretical foundation.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a deeper understanding of experimental methods, helping practitioners/researchers in either domain apply variance reduction approaches more effectively.

title: CAR: Cross-Vehicle Kinodynamics Adaptation via Mobility Representation
summary: Developing autonomous off-road mobility typically requires either extensive, platform-specific data collection or relies on simplified abstractions, such as unicycle or bicycle models, that fail to capture the complex kinodynamics of diverse platforms, ranging from wheeled to tracked vehicles. This limitation hinders scalability across evolving heterogeneous autonomous robot fleets. To address this challenge, we propose Cross-vehicle kinodynamics Adaptation via mobility Representation (CAR), a novel framework that enables rapid mobility transfer to new vehicles. CAR employs a Transformer encoder with Adaptive Layer Normalization to embed vehicle trajectory transitions and physical configurations into a shared mobility latent space. By identifying and extracting commonality from nearest neighbors within this latent space, our approach enables rapid kinodynamics adaptation to novel platforms with minimal data collection and computational overhead. We evaluate CAR using the Verti-Bench simulator, built on the Chrono multi-physics engine, and validate its performance on four distinct physical configurations of the Verti-4-Wheeler platform. With only one minute of new trajectory data, CAR achieves up to 67.2% reduction in prediction error compared to direct neighbor transfer across diverse unseen vehicle configurations, demonstrating the effectiveness of cross-vehicle mobility knowledge transfer in both simulated and real-world environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CAR, a framework for rapidly transferring mobility knowledge to different robotic vehicles using minimal data and computation.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in platform-specific data collection and limitations of simplified kinodynamic models for diverse robot fleets.
AI.method: CAR uses a Transformer encoder with Adaptive Layer Normalization to represent vehicle kinodynamics in a shared latent space, enabling mobility adaptation based on commonalities across vehicles.
AI.result: CAR shows a 67.2% reduction in prediction error with only one minute of new data when tested on various vehicle configurations in simulations.
AI.conclusion: CAR effectively enables cross-vehicle knowledge transfer, demonstrating scalability and adaptability for autonomous robot fleets in diverse environments.

title: Correlation Analysis of Generative Models
summary: Based on literature review about existing diffusion models and flow matching with a neural network to predict a predefined target from noisy data, a unified representation is first proposed for these models using two simple linear equations in this paper. Theoretical analysis of the proposed model is then presented. Our theoretical analysis shows that the correlation between the noisy data and the predicted target is sometimes weak in the existing diffusion models and flow matching. This might affect the prediction (or learning) process which plays a crucial role in all models.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes diffusion models and flow matching's efficacy in predicting targets from noisy data, introducing a unified representation and uncovering weak correlations in existing methods that could impair learning.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing diffusion models and flow matching by identifying their weaknesses in predicting targets from noisy data.
AI.method: Proposes a unified representation using two linear equations and conducts theoretical analysis on the correlation between noisy data and predicted targets.
AI.result: Finds that existing models sometimes have weak correlations between noisy data and targets, potentially affecting the prediction and learning processes.
AI.conclusion: A unified model shows that current diffusion methods might be limited by low correlation in prediction processes, highlighting an area for improvement.

title: Empowering Locally Deployable Medical Agent via State Enhanced Logical Skills for FHIR-based Clinical Tasks
summary: While Large Language Models demonstrate immense potential as proactive Medical Agents, their real-world deployment is severely bottlenecked by data scarcity under privacy constraints. To overcome this, we propose State-Enhanced Logical-Skill Memory (SELSM), a training-free framework that distills simulated clinical trajectories into entity-agnostic operational rules within an abstract skill space. During inference, a Query-Anchored Two-Stage Retrieval mechanism dynamically fetches these entity-agnostic logical priors to guide the agent's step-by-step reasoning, effectively resolving the state polysemy problem. Evaluated on MedAgentBench -- the only authoritative high-fidelity virtual EHR sandbox benchmarked with real clinical data -- SELSM substantially elevates the zero-shot capabilities of locally deployable foundation models (30B--32B parameters). Notably, on the Qwen3-30B-A3B backbone, our framework completely eliminates task chain breakdowns to achieve a 100\% completion rate, boosting the overall success rate by an absolute 22.67\% and significantly outperforming existing memory-augmented baselines. This study demonstrates that equipping models with a dynamically updatable, state-enhanced cognitive scaffold is a privacy-preserving and computationally efficient pathway for local adaptation of AI agents to clinical information systems. While currently validated on FHIR-based EHR interactions as an initial step, the entity-agnostic design of SELSM provides a principled foundation toward broader clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SELSM, a training-free framework to enhance medical AI agents in privacy-constrained environments by dynamically leveraging logical priors, achieving superior performance on MedAgentBench.
AI.motivation: The scarcity of data and privacy constraints bottleneck the deployment of AI models as proactive medical agents in real-world clinical settings.
AI.method: SELSM distills clinical trajectories into abstract operational rules via a Query-Anchored Two-Stage Retrieval mechanism to guide inference and solve state polysemy.
AI.result: SELSM significantly elevates zero-shot capabilities of foundation models, achieving 100% completion rate on Qwen3-30B-A3B and a 22.67% improvement in the success rate.
AI.conclusion: The study shows SELSM as a privacy-preserving and efficient framework for locally adapting AI agents to clinical systems, with potential broader applications in clinical deployment.

title: calibfusion: Transformer-Based Differentiable Calibration for Radar-Camera Fusion Detection in Water-Surface Environments
summary:
Millimeter-wave (mmWave) Radar--Camera fusion improves perception under adverse illumination and weather, but its performance is sensitive to Radar--Camera extrinsic calibration: residual misalignment biases Radar-to-image projection and degrades cross-modal aggregation for downstream 2D detection. Existing calibration and auto-calibration methods are mainly developed for road and urban scenes with abundant structures and object constraints, whereas water-surface environments feature large textureless regions, sparse and intermittent targets, and wave-/specular-induced Radar clutter, which weakens explicit object-centric matching.
  We propose CalibFusion, a calibration-conditioned Radar--Camera fusion detector that learns implicit extrinsic refinement end-to-end with the detection objective. CalibFusion builds a multi-frame persistence-aware Radar density representation with intensity weighting and Doppler-guided suppression of fast-varying clutter. A cross-modal transformer interaction module predicts a confidence-gated refinement of the initial extrinsics, which is integrated through a differentiable projection-and-splatting operator to generate calibration-conditioned image-plane Radar features.
  Experiments on WaterScenes and FLOW show improved fusion-based 2D detection and robustness under synthetic miscalibration, supported by sensitivity analyses and qualitative Radar-to-image overlays. Results on nuScenes indicate that the refinement mechanism transfers beyond water-surface scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CalibFusion, a Radar-Camera fusion system tailored for challenging environments to enhance 2D detection by refining extrinsic calibration through end-to-end learning.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of Radar-Camera fusion in water-surface environments, where traditional calibration methods struggle due to large textureless areas, sparse targets, and clutter.
AI.method: The proposed CalibFusion system employs persistence-aware Radar representation, Doppler-based clutter suppression, and a cross-modal transformer for refining extrinsics, with integration achieved through a differentiable projection operator.
AI.result: CalibFusion improves 2D detection under miscalibration in benchmarks like WaterScenes and FLOW, with successful transferability demonstrated on nuScenes.
AI.conclusion: The integration of extrinsic refinement with detection objectives provides robust Radar-Camera fusion in adverse environments, enhancing multi-modal perception capabilities.

title: SafarDB: FPGA-Accelerated Distributed Transactions via Replicated Data Types
summary: Data replication is a critical aspect of data center design, as it ensures high availability, scalability, and fault tolerance. However, replicas need to be coordinated to maintain convergence and database integrity constraints under transactional workloads. Commutative Replicated Data Types (RDTs) provide convergence for conflict-free objects using relaxed consistency, and Well-coordinated Replicated Data Types (WRDTs) provide convergence and integrity for general objects using a hybrid model, relaxed when possible and strong when necessary. While state-of-the-art hardware acceleration of RDT uses Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA), we observe that trends towards lower latency and higher throughput have driven recent data center architectures to leverage FPGAs as application accelerators. In contrast to deploying an FPGA-based Smart NIC, this paper connects an FPGA accelerator card directly to the network, which allows a complete redesign of the NIC to match the needs of the FPGA-hosted application. We co-design a network-attached FPGA replication engine with an FPGA-resident network interface, enabling near-network execution of replicated transactions and direct invocation of FPGA-resident operators. Following this approach, we introduce SafarDB, FPGA-accelerated Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) and WRDTs. SafarDB accelerates both relaxed and strongly ordered replication paths; when strong ordering is required, SafarDB accelerates the underlying consensus control path. SafarDB improves CRDT latency and throughput by 7.0X and 5.3X, and WRDT latency and throughput by 12X and 6.8X compared to a state-of-the-art RDMA-based implementation. Further, experiments demonstrate that SafarDB is more resilient to crash-failures than existing CPU/RDMA-based CRDT and WRDT implementations, and SafarDB can detect leader failures and elect new leaders much faster than previously possible.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SafarDB, an FPGA-based engine improving data replication performance and resilience over traditional RDMA-based methods.
AI.motivation: To enhance data replication in data centers by leveraging FPGA accelerators instead of RDMA, achieving better latency, throughput, and resilience.
AI.method: Designing an FPGA-attached network interface and developing SafarDB, which accelerates replication for CRDTs and WRDTs by combining near-network execution and FPGA-based consensus protocols.
AI.result: SafarDB provides up to 12X latency improvement, 6.8X throughput boost, and greater fault tolerance compared to RDMA-based replication systems.
AI.conclusion: The FPGA-based SafarDB significantly advances replication performance, especially in strong ordering scenarios, offering an effective alternative for modern data centers.

title: The role of team diversity in AI systems development
summary: The widespread integration of AI technologies has intensified concerns about fairness and bias, as these systems often perpetuate societal inequalities through flawed data and design choices. While software engineering research has largely concentrated on technical solutions, such as improving datasets and models, the social dynamics that shape AI outcomes remain underexplored. This study investigates the role of team diversity in the development of AI systems. Drawing from the experience of four AI focused teams working in a large software company operating in Brazil and Portugal, and collaborating with global clients, the study explores how diverse teams influence the development of AI systems. Using Grounded Theory, we conducted 25 interviews with software professionals involved in projects spanning domains such as education, energy, accessibility, and facial recognition. Although our study is conducted in an organizational setting, the variety of projects, from regional to multinational, ensures exposure to global development practices and diverse team dynamics, bringing a variety of perspectives into our findings. Our analysis revealed six key roles that team diversity played in AI development: diversifying perspectives for bias identification, bringing empathy to AI development, addressing systemic discrimination, supporting inclusive and participatory decision making, using diversity as a safeguard against bias, and fostering broadened thinking in problem solving. These findings highlight the importance of incorporating diverse perspectives in AI projects and offer practical recommendations for integrating fairness considerations into software development practices.

AI.tldr: The study addresses how team diversity impacts fairness and bias in AI by analyzing the experiences of diverse teams in developing AI systems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the role of team diversity in addressing fairness and bias during AI system development, as societal inequalities are often perpetuated in current AI due to flawed data and design.
AI.method: The study employs a Grounded Theory approach based on 25 interviews with software professionals involved in various AI projects in a large software company operating in Brazil and Portugal.
AI.result: The analysis identified six roles of team diversity: mitigating bias, fostering empathy, combating systemic discrimination, enabling inclusive decision-making, safeguarding against bias, and encouraging broader problem-solving approaches.
AI.conclusion: Team diversity is essential in developing fair and unbiased AI systems. The findings provide practical recommendations to integrate diverse perspectives and fairness into software development.

title: A Systematic Investigation of Document Chunking Strategies and Embedding Sensitivity
summary: We present the first large-scale, cross-domain evaluation of document chunking strategies for dense retrieval, addressing a critical but underexplored aspect of retrieval-augmented systems. In our study, 36 segmentation methods spanning fixed-size, semantic, structure-aware, hierarchical, adaptive, and LLM-assisted approaches are benchmarked across six diverse knowledge domains using five different embedding models. Retrieval performance is assessed using graded relevance scores from a state-of-the-art LLM evaluator, with Normalised DCG@5 as the primary metric (complemented by Hit@5 and MRR). Our experiments show that content-aware chunking significantly improves retrieval effectiveness over naive fixed-length splitting. The top-performing strategy, Paragraph Group Chunking, achieved the highest overall accuracy (mean nDCG@5~0.459) and substantially better top-rank hit rates (Precision@1~24%, Hit@5~59%). In contrast, simple fixed-size character chunking as baselines performed poorly (nDCG@5 < 0.244, Precision@1~2-3%). We observe pronounced domain-specific differences: dynamic token sizing is strongest in biology, physics and health, while paragraph grouping is strongest in legal and maths. Larger embedding models yield higher absolute scores but remain sensitive to suboptimal segmentation, indicating that better chunking and large embeddings provide complementary benefits. In addition to accuracy gains, we quantify the efficiency trade-offs of advanced chunking. Producing more, smaller chunks can increase index size and latency. Consequently, we identify methods (like dynamic chunking) that approach an optimal balance of effectiveness and efficiency. These findings establish chunking as a vital lever for improving retrieval performance and reliability.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates 36 document chunking strategies for dense retrieval across six domains using five embedding models. It finds content-aware chunking substantially outperforms fixed-size splitting, with Paragraph Group Chunking achieving the highest accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore and benchmark various document chunking strategies to optimize retrieval-augmented systems, a critical but under-researched area.
AI.method: 36 segmentation methods were tested across six domains and five embedding models. Retrieval performance was analyzed using metrics like nDCG@5, Precision@1, and MRR, while also considering efficiency trade-offs.
AI.result: Content-aware chunking showed significant improvement in retrieval effectiveness. Paragraph Group Chunking achieved mean nDCG@5 of ~0.459, significantly better than fixed-size chunking (<0.244). Domain-specific preferences were noted, and larger embedding models improved performance but remained sensitive to segmentation quality.
AI.conclusion: Chunking strategy plays a critical role in improving document retrieval systems. Methods like dynamic token sizing and paragraph grouping improve performance, and an optimal balance between accuracy and efficiency is necessary for practical applications.

title: Generative Adversarial Regression (GAR): Learning Conditional Risk Scenarios
summary: We propose Generative Adversarial Regression (GAR), a framework for learning conditional risk scenarios through generators aligned with downstream risk objectives. GAR builds on a regression characterization of conditional risk for elicitable functionals, including quantiles, expectiles, and jointly elicitable pairs. We extend this principle from point prediction to generative modeling by training generators whose policy-induced risk matches that of real data under the same context. To ensure robustness across all policies, GAR adopts a minimax formulation in which an adversarial policy identifies worst-case discrepancies in risk evaluation while the generator adapts to eliminate them. This structure preserves alignment with the risk functional across a broad class of policies rather than a fixed, pre-specified set. We illustrate GAR through a tail-risk instantiation based on jointly elicitable $(\mathrm{VaR}, \mathrm{ES})$ objectives. Experiments on S\&P 500 data show that GAR produces scenarios that better preserve downstream risk than unconditional, econometric, and direct predictive baselines while remaining stable under adversarially selected policies.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Generative Adversarial Regression (GAR) framework for creating conditional risk scenarios using generator models aligned with risk objectives.
AI.motivation: To improve the generation of conditional risk scenarios with alignment to downstream risk measures and robustness in policy evaluation.
AI.method: The GAR framework leverages generator models characterized by conditional risk for specific functionals (e.g., quantiles, expectiles). It uses a minimax approach involving generators and adversarial policies to ensure alignment with risk functionals across various policies.
AI.result: GAR demonstrates the ability to generate scenarios that align better with downstream risk, particularly in tail-risk contexts such as $(\mathrm{VaR}, \mathrm{ES})$, outperforming other baselines in experiments with S\&P 500 data.
AI.conclusion: GAR introduces a robust, effective, and risk-aligned method for generating conditional scenarios, showing significant potential in risk assessment and predictive tasks.

title: Material Driven HRI Design: Aesthetics as Explainability
comment: 4 pages, 1 table, 2026 ACM/IEEE Human-Robot Interaction Conference Workshop on Articulating the Value of Design Research for HRI
summary: Aesthetics - often treated as secondary to function-guides how people interpret robots' roles. A great deal of robot designs - both real and fictitious - use sleek industrial aesthetics. These feature hard glossy plastics, hiding as much of the underlying mechanical and electrical components as possible, resembling something akin to a nude humanoid figure. This leaves robots as something of a blank slate to which end-users apply coverings to, often based on media of fiction and non-fiction alike. We argue that designers can take cues from fashion to design interaction and set appropriate expectations. Rather than viewing appearance as decoration, we propose that color, texture, and material choices function as interaction signals. These signals can invite or discourage touch, clarify a robot's role, and help align user expectations with a robot's actual capabilities. When done thoughtfully, such cues can create familiarity and legibility; when done poorly, they can lead to wrong expectations. This preliminary paper proposes a framework describing how materials can create explainability by signaling expectations for interaction, task, and environment. We use this framework to do a content analysis of 6 robots.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how aesthetics, inspired by fashion, can influence robot design by using materials, colors, and textures as interaction signals to set user expectations and improve familiarity.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the observation that robot designs often rely on sleek industrial aesthetics, which may not clearly communicate their roles or functions. The goal is to identify how aesthetics can serve as a tool for interaction design, aligning user expectations with robot capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework for using materials, color, and texture in robot design to create explainability and signal interaction expectations. They perform a content analysis of 6 robots to test this approach.
AI.result: The paper suggests that design choices, such as materials and textures, can serve as signals that help clarify a robot's role and create better alignment with user expectations.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating aesthetic design elements thoughtfully can enhance a robot's interaction clarity and encourage appropriate expectations, while neglecting these elements risks creating misunderstandings between users and robots.

title: Annealed Co-Generation: Disentangling Variables via Progressive Pairwise Modeling
summary: For multivariate co-generation in scientific applications, we advocate pairwise block rather than joint modeling of all variables. This design mitigates the computational burden and data imbalance. To this end, we propose an Annealed Co-Generation (ACG) framework that replaces high-dimensional diffusion modeling with a low-dimensional diffusion model, which enables multivariate co-generation by composing pairwise variable generations. We first train an unconditional diffusion model over causal variables that are disentangled into pairs. At inference time, we recover the joint distribution by coupling these pairwise models through shared common variables, enabling coherent multivariate generation without any additional training. By employing a three-stage annealing process-Consensus, Heating, and Cooling-our method enforces consistency across shared common variables and progressively constrains each pairwise data distribution to lie on a learnable manifold, while maintaining high likelihood within each pair. We demonstrate the framework's flexibility and efficacy on two distinct scientific tasks: flow-field completion and antibody generation. All datasets and code will be made publicly available upon publication.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for generating multivariate data using pairwise modeling instead of joint modeling to reduce computational challenges.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome computational burdens and data imbalance challenges in multivariate co-generation by replacing high-dimensional modeling with low-dimensional approaches.
AI.method: The proposed Annealed Co-Generation (ACG) framework uses diffusion models for pairwise variable generation, involving a three-stage annealing process to ensure consistency and high likelihood of generated data.
AI.result: The ACG framework demonstrates flexibility and effectiveness in scientific tasks like flow-field completion and antibody generation, validated through experiments.
AI.conclusion: Pairwise modeling via the ACG framework provides a viable solution for multivariate co-generation, achieving coherence and computational efficiency without additional training efforts.

title: Enhancing Web Agents with a Hierarchical Memory Tree
summary: Large language model-based web agents have shown strong potential in automating web interactions through advanced reasoning and instruction following. While retrieval-based memory derived from historical trajectories enables these agents to handle complex, long-horizon tasks, current methods struggle to generalize across unseen websites. We identify that this challenge arises from the flat memory structures that entangle high-level task logic with site-specific action details. This entanglement induces a workflow mismatch in new environments, where retrieved contents are conflated with current web, leading to logically inconsistent execution. To address this, we propose Hierarchical Memory Tree (HMT), a structured framework designed to explicitly decouple logical planning from action execution. HMT constructs a three-level hierarchy from raw trajectories via an automated abstraction pipeline: the Intent level maps diverse user instructions to standardized task goals; the Stage level defines reusable semantic subgoals characterized by observable pre-conditions and post-conditions; and the Action level stores action patterns paired with transferable semantic element descriptions. Leveraging this structure, we develop a stage-aware inference mechanism comprising a Planner and an Actor. By explicitly validating pre-conditions, the Planner aligns the current state with the correct logical subgoal to prevent workflow mismatch, while the Actor grounds actions by matching the stored semantic descriptions to the target page. Experimental results on Mind2Web and WebArena show that HMT significantly outperforms flat-memory methods, particularly in cross-website and cross-domain scenarios, highlighting the necessity of structured memory for robust generalization of web agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Hierarchical Memory Tree (HMT) framework to improve large language model-based web agents by decoupling task logic from site-specific actions, enhancing cross-website generalization.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of current web agents that struggle in generalizing across unseen websites due to flat memory structures causing logical inconsistencies.
AI.method: The proposed HMT uses a three-level hierarchy (Intent, Stage, Action) to separate logical planning from action execution, leveraging an abstraction pipeline to structure memory. It also develops a Planner and Actor mechanism for proper task execution.
AI.result: The HMT framework significantly outperformed flat-memory methods in cross-website and cross-domain scenarios tested on Mind2Web and WebArena benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: HMT addresses workflow mismatches by structuring memory and validating logical preconditions, proving structured memory is essential for robust web agent generalization.

title: Does Semantic Noise Initialization Transfer from Images to Videos? A Paired Diagnostic Study
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Multimodal Intelligence: Next Token Prediction & Beyond
summary: Semantic noise initialization has been reported to improve robustness and controllability in image diffusion models. Whether these gains transfer to text-to-video (T2V) generation remains unclear, since temporal coupling can introduce extra degrees of freedom and instability. We benchmark semantic noise initialization against standard Gaussian noise using a frozen VideoCrafter-style T2V diffusion backbone and VBench on 100 prompts. Using prompt-level paired tests with bootstrap confidence intervals and a sign-flip permutation test, we observe a small positive trend on temporal-related dimensions; however, the 95 percent confidence interval includes zero (p ~ 0.17) and the overall score remains on par with the baseline. To understand this outcome, we analyze the induced perturbations in noise space and find patterns consistent with weak or unstable signal. We recommend prompt-level paired evaluation and noise-space diagnostics as standard practice when studying initialization schemes for T2V diffusion.

AI.tldr: The study tests semantic noise initialization in text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models, finding limited improvement over standard Gaussian noise with weak or unstable signal patterns.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether semantic noise initialization, which helps in image diffusion models, offers similar benefits for text-to-video (T2V) generation, given the complexities introduced by temporal coupling.
AI.method: The authors benchmark semantic noise against Gaussian noise using a frozen VideoCrafter T2V diffusion model and evaluate with VBench on 100 prompts. Statistical tests, including bootstrap intervals and permutation tests, are performed to assess performance.
AI.result: Results showed a small positive trend in temporal dimensions but the overall score remains comparable to the baseline with 95% confidence intervals including zero. Weak or unstable patterns were observed in the noise space perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Semantic noise initialization does not demonstrate significant advantages for T2V generation, and better methods for analyzing initialization schemes are recommended, including prompt-level paired evaluations and noise diagnostics.

title: Covenant-72B: Pre-Training a 72B LLM with Trustless Peers Over-the-Internet
summary: Recently, there has been increased interest in globally distributed training, which has the promise to both reduce training costs and democratize participation in building large-scale foundation models. However, existing models trained in a globally distributed manner are relatively small in scale and have only been trained with whitelisted participants. Therefore, they do not yet realize the full promise of democratized participation. In this report, we describe Covenant-72B, an LLM produced by the largest collaborative globally distributed pre-training run (in terms of both compute and model scale), which simultaneously allowed open, permissionless participation supported by a live blockchain protocol. We utilized a state-of-the-art communication-efficient optimizer, SparseLoCo, supporting dynamic participation with peers joining and leaving freely. Our model, pre-trained on approximately 1.1T tokens, performs competitively with fully centralized models pre-trained on similar or higher compute budgets, demonstrating that fully democratized, non-whitelisted participation is not only feasible, but can be achieved at unprecedented scale for a globally distributed pre-training run.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Covenant-72B, a large-scale language model created through global, collaborative training using a blockchain protocol for open participation.
AI.motivation: To explore whether globally distributed training with open participation can produce competitive large-scale language models and democratize model development.
AI.method: Implemented the SparseLoCo optimizer to enable communication-efficient, dynamic participation during globally distributed training using a blockchain protocol.
AI.result: Covenant-72B was trained on 1.1 trillion tokens and showed competitive performance compared to models trained in centralized setups with similar or greater compute budgets.
AI.conclusion: Globally distributed training with non-whitelisted and open participation is feasible at an unprecedented scale, demonstrating its potential in building competitive foundational models.

title: IOTEL: A Tool for Generating IoT-enriched Object-Centric Event Logs
summary: Integrating Internet of Things (IoT) data with business process event logs is crucial for analysing IoT-enhanced processes, yet remains challenging due to differences in abstraction levels and the separation of data sources. Simply incorporating raw IoT data increases the size and complexity of the resulting log, often requiring additional processing before process analysis can be performed. While tools for generating IoT-enriched event logs exist, they either rely on specialised schemas or focus on extracting event logs from sensor data, offering limited support for integrating process-relevant IoT data into existing event logs. To address this gap, we present IOTEL, a tool for systematically generating IoT-enriched object-centric event logs (OCEL). By building on the OCEL schema, IOTEL enables structured IoT data integration compatible with existing process mining tools. It support practitioners and researchers in analysing IoT-enhanced business processes, as demonstrated in a real-world scenario. A video demonstrating the tool is available online.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IOTEL, a tool designed to systematically integrate IoT data into event logs for process analysis while resolving challenges of data separation and abstraction.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome challenges in integrating IoT data with event logs for analysing IoT-enhanced business processes, as existing tools either rely on specialised schemas or have limited integration capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose IOTEL, a tool based on the OCEL schema, to integrate structured IoT data into event logs while ensuring compatibility with process mining tools.
AI.result: IOTEL successfully generates IoT-enriched object-centric event logs and its utility is demonstrated in a real-world scenario.
AI.conclusion: IOTEL facilitates systematic IoT data integration, helping researchers and practitioners analyze IoT-enhanced processes effectively.

title: Can Safety Emerge from Weak Supervision? A Systematic Analysis of Small Language Models
summary: Safety alignment is critical for deploying large language models (LLMs) in real-world applications, yet most existing approaches rely on large human-annotated datasets and static red-teaming benchmarks that are costly, difficult to scale, and slow to adapt to evolving model behaviors. Moreover, overly conservative safety mechanisms can reduce model usefulness by rejecting sensitive but legitimate queries. We introduce Self-MOA (Self Multi-Objective Alignment), a fully automated framework for aligning small language models using weak supervision from automated evaluator models. Self-MOA operates as a closed loop that dynamically generates model-specific red team prompts, constructs preference data from model-generated responses, and aligns models via multi-objective preference optimization to jointly optimize for safety and helpfulness. Across multiple small language models and safety benchmarks, Self-MOA achieves a 12.41\% improvement in safety while preserving helpfulness, using as little as 11 times less training data than human-supervised alignment baselines. These results demonstrate that adaptive, automated alignment can reduce the dependence on static, human-curated safety pipelines in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Self-MOA, an automated framework to align small language models for safety and helpfulness with minimal reliance on human-annotated datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing safety alignment approaches for LLMs rely heavily on costly, static, and human-annotated datasets, which hinder scalability and adaptability.
AI.method: Self-MOA uses a closed-loop system of model-specific prompts to generate preference data from responses and performs multi-objective preference optimization for simultaneous safety and helpfulness.
AI.result: Self-MOA improves safety by 12.41% while maintaining helpfulness, using significantly less training data compared to human-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that automated safety alignment methodologies can reduce dependency on static, human-curated processes, making them suitable for resource-constrained applications.

title: Momentum SVGD-EM for Accelerated Maximum Marginal Likelihood Estimation
summary: Maximum marginal likelihood estimation (MMLE) can be formulated as the optimization of a free energy functional. From this viewpoint, the Expectation-Maximisation (EM) algorithm admits a natural interpretation as a coordinate descent method over the joint space of model parameters and probability measures. Recently, a significant body of work has adopted this perspective, leading to interacting particle algorithms for MMLE. In this paper, we propose an accelerated version of one such procedure, based on Stein variational gradient descent (SVGD), by introducing Nesterov acceleration in both the parameter updates and in the space of probability measures. The resulting method, termed Momentum SVGD-EM, consistently accelerates convergence in terms of required iterations across various tasks of increasing difficulty, demonstrating effectiveness in both low- and high-dimensional settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Momentum SVGD-EM, an accelerated algorithm for MMLE using Nesterov acceleration in parameters and probability measures.
AI.motivation: To enhance MMLE optimization processes by introducing acceleration mechanisms, addressing convergence efficiency in both low- and high-dimensional problems.
AI.method: The authors integrate Stein variational gradient descent (SVGD) with Nesterov acceleration for parameter updates and probability measures, forming Momentum SVGD-EM.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves faster convergence across tasks in terms of iterations in various dimensional environments.
AI.conclusion: Momentum SVGD-EM proves to consistently improve convergence rates, showcasing its utility for MMLE optimization in diverse problem settings.

title: VertiAdaptor: Online Kinodynamics Adaptation for Vertically Challenging Terrain
summary: Autonomous driving in off-road environments presents significant challenges due to the dynamic and unpredictable nature of unstructured terrain. Traditional kinodynamic models often struggle to generalize across diverse geometric and semantic terrain types, underscoring the need for real-time adaptation to ensure safe and reliable navigation. We propose VertiAdaptor (VA), a novel online adaptation framework that efficiently integrates elevation with semantic embeddings to enable terrain-aware kinodynamic modeling and planning via function encoders. VA learns a kinodynamic space spanned by a set of neural ordinary differential equation basis functions, capturing complex vehicle-terrain interactions across varied environments. After offline training, the proposed approach can rapidly adapt to new, unseen environments by identifying kinodynamics in the learned space through a computationally efficient least-squares calculation. We evaluate VA within the Verti-Bench simulator, built on the Chrono multi-physics engine, and validate its performance both in simulation and on a physical Verti-4-Wheeler platform. Our results demonstrate that VA improves prediction accuracy by up to 23.9% and achieves a 5X faster adaptation time, advancing the robustness and reliability of autonomous robots in complex and evolving off-road environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VertiAdaptor, an online adaptation framework that enhances autonomous driving in off-road terrains by integrating elevation with semantic embeddings for terrain-aware modeling and planning, achieving improved prediction accuracy and faster adaptation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the difficulty of autonomous driving in off-road terrains due to their dynamic and unpredictable nature, where traditional models fail to generalize across diverse terrains.
AI.method: The method involves using VertiAdaptor (VA), which combines elevation and semantic embeddings for kinodynamic modeling using function encoders and neural ordinary differential equation basis functions. It enables rapid adaptation via efficient least-squares calculation.
AI.result: The results show that VA improves prediction accuracy by up to 23.9% and achieves a 5X faster adaptation time, confirmed through simulation and physical validation.
AI.conclusion: VertiAdaptor advances the robustness and reliability of autonomous vehicles in complex off-road environments by enabling efficient and accurate terrain-aware adaptation.

title: RACER: Risk-Aware Calibrated Efficient Routing for Large Language Models
summary: Efficiently routing queries to the optimal large language model (LLM) is crucial for optimizing the cost-performance trade-off in multi-model systems. However, most existing routers rely on single-model selection, making them susceptible to misrouting. In this work, we formulate LLM routing as the $α$-VOR problem to minimize expected set size while controlling the misrouting risk, and propose a novel method -- RACER, extending base routers to output model sets that can be subsequently aggregated for improved output. In particular, RACER constructs nested model sets via augmented scoring and utilizes finite-sample concentration bounds to calibrate a threshold that allows for both variable set sizes and abstention. We theoretically prove that RACER achieves rigorous distribution-free risk control on unseen test data in a post-hoc and model-agnostic manner. Extensive experiments verify our theoretical guarantees and demonstrate that RACER consistently enhances downstream accuracy across a wide range of benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RACER, a method for better routing queries in multi-model systems by outputting model sets instead of single models, enhancing performance and controlling misrouting risks.
AI.motivation: Current query routing systems often misroute tasks by relying on single-model selection, leading to inefficiencies. A better routing mechanism is needed for improved cost-performance balance.
AI.method: The authors propose RACER, which uses nested model sets and augments scoring to calibrate thresholds, enabling variable model selection sizes and abstention, ensuring risk control.
AI.result: RACER is proved theoretically to ensure distribution-free risk control and tested experimentally to consistently improve accuracy across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: RACER improves query routing in LLM-based systems by enhancing accuracy and minimizing misrouting risks, providing a mechanism for theoretical and practical reliability.

title: Self-Supervised Multi-Modal World Model with 4D Space-Time Embedding
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 1 table. Presented at 2026 World Modeling Workshop, Mila Quebec
summary: We present DeepEarth, a self-supervised multi-modal world model with Earth4D, a novel planetary-scale 4D space-time positional encoder. Earth4D extends 3D multi-resolution hash encoding to include time, efficiently scaling across the planet over centuries with sub-meter, sub-second precision. Multi-modal encoders (e.g. vision-language models) are fused with Earth4D embeddings and trained via masked reconstruction. We demonstrate Earth4D's expressive power by achieving state-of-the-art performance on an ecological forecasting benchmark. Earth4D with learnable hash probing surpasses a multi-modal foundation model pre-trained on substantially more data. Access open source code and download models at: https://github.com/legel/deepearth

AI.tldr: DeepEarth is a self-supervised multi-modal world model leveraging Earth4D, a novel positional encoder for planetary-scale space-time applications, achieving state-of-the-art ecological forecasting results.
AI.motivation: To create a robust, scalable world model capable of capturing planetary-scale spacetime data for applications like environmental forecasting and modeling through multi-modal embeddings.
AI.method: The authors propose Earth4D, a 4D positional encoder that extends 3D hash encodings to include time, achieving fine spatial and temporal precision. They integrate multi-modal encoders (e.g., vision-language models) with Earth4D, training them using masked reconstruction methods for tasks such as ecological forecasting.
AI.result: Earth4D achieves state-of-the-art performance on an ecological forecasting benchmark. It outperforms other models, including a multi-modal foundation model pre-trained on much larger datasets, showing its effectiveness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Earth4D demonstrates the potential for precise and scalable spacetime modeling using multi-modal embeddings. The open-source release of the model and code provides opportunities for further advancements and exploration.

title: Unmixing microinfrared spectroscopic images of cross-sections of historical oil paintings
summary:
Spectroscopic imaging (SI) has become central to heritage science because it enables non-invasive, spatially resolved characterisation of materials in artefacts. In particular, attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared microscopy (ATR-$μ$FTIR) is widely used to analyse painting cross-sections, where a spectrum is recorded at each pixel to form a hyperspectral image (HSI). Interpreting these data is difficult: spectra are often mixtures of several species in heterogeneous, multi-layered and degraded samples, and current practice still relies heavily on manual comparison with reference libraries. This workflow is slow, subjective and hard to scale.
  We propose an unsupervised CNN autoencoder for blind unmixing of ATR-$μ$FTIR HSIs, estimating endmember spectra and their abundance maps while exploiting local spatial structure through patch-based modelling. To reduce sensitivity to atmospheric and acquisition artefacts across $>1500$ bands, we introduce a weighted spectral angle distance (WSAD) loss with automatic band-reliability weights derived from robust measures of spatial flatness, neighbour agreement and spectral roughness. Compared with standard SAD training, WSAD improves interpretability in contamination-prone spectral regions. We demonstrate the method on an ATR-$μ$FTIR cross-section from the Ghent Altarpiece attributed to the Van Eyck brothers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an unsupervised CNN autoencoder to enhance interpretation of hyperspectral images (HSIs) in heritage science, specifically for analyzing painting cross-sections.
AI.motivation: Analyzing ATR-$μ$FTIR hyperspectral images (HSIs) of painting cross-sections is challenging due to spectral mixture complexity, degradation, and reliance on subjective manual comparison.
AI.method: An unsupervised CNN autoencoder is proposed for blind unmixing, incorporating patch-based modeling and a weighted spectral angle distance (WSAD) loss to improve band reliability during spectral analysis.
AI.result: WSAD enhances interpretation by reducing sensitivity to contamination-prone spectral regions and providing more reliable endmember spectra and abundance maps.
AI.conclusion: The method improves scalability and objectivity in interpreting ATR-$μ$FTIR HSIs, demonstrated on a cross-section from the Ghent Altarpiece.

title: A Hodge-Based Framework for Service Operational Analysis in Serverless Platforms
summary: In this paper we propose a method for analyzing services deployed in serverless platforms. These services typically consists of orchestrated functions that can exhibit complex and non-conservative information flows due to the interaction of independently deployed functions under coarse-grained control mechanisms. We introduce a topological model of serverless services and make use of the Hodge decomposition to partition observed operational flows into locally correctable components and globally persistent harmonic modes. Our analysis shows that harmonic flows naturally arise from different kind of interactions among functions and should be interpreted as structural properties of serverless systems rather than configuration errors. We present a systematic methodology for analyzing inter-function flows and deriving actionable remediation strategies, including dumping effects to contain the effects of harmonic inefficiencies as an alternative to completely restructure the topological model of the service. Experimental results confirm that the proposed approach can uncover latent architectural structures leading to inefficiencies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method for analyzing serverless platform services to identify and manage complex information flows using topological modeling and Hodge decomposition.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of complex and inefficient information flows in serverless platforms caused by orchestrating independently deployed functions with coarse-grained control.
AI.method: A topological model of serverless services using Hodge decomposition is applied to partition operational flows and identify structural inefficiencies within serverless systems.
AI.result: The analysis highlights how harmonic flows reveal underlying inefficiencies and structural properties in serverless systems, leading to actionable strategies to remediate inefficiencies.
AI.conclusion: The methodology effectively identifies architectural inefficiencies in serverless platforms and offers strategies to mitigate them without drastic system restructuring.

title: SWE-Fuse: Empowering Software Agents via Issue-free Trajectory Learning and Entropy-aware RLVR Training
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have transformed the software engineering landscape. Recently, numerous LLM-based agents have been developed to address real-world software issue fixing tasks. Despite their state-of-the-art performance, Despite achieving state-of-the-art performance, these agents face a significant challenge: \textbf{Insufficient high-quality issue descriptions.} Real-world datasets often exhibit misalignments between issue descriptions and their corresponding solutions, introducing noise and ambiguity that mislead automated agents and limit their problem-solving effectiveness. We propose \textbf{\textit{SWE-Fuse}}, an issue-description-aware training framework that fuses issue-description-guided and issue-free samples for training SWE agents. It consists of two key modules: (1) An issue-free-driven trajectory learning module for mitigating potentially misleading issue descriptions while enabling the model to learn step-by-step debugging processes; and (2) An entropy-aware RLVR training module, which adaptively adjusts training dynamics through entropy-driven clipping. It applies relaxed clipping under high entropy to encourage exploration, and stricter clipping under low entropy to ensure training stability. We evaluate SWE-Fuse on the widely studied SWE-bench Verified benchmark shows to demonstrate its effectiveness in solving real-world software problems. Specifically, SWE-Fuse outperforms the best 8B and 32B baselines by 43.0\% and 60.2\% in solve rate, respectively. Furthermore, integrating SWE-Fuse with test-time scaling (TTS) enables further performance improvements, achieving solve rates of 49.8\% and 65.2\% under TTS@8 for the 8B and 32B models, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SWE-Fuse, a framework designed to enhance issue fixing tasks for large language model-based agents by mitigating noise in issue descriptions, and it demonstrates improved solve rates significantly over baselines.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of insufficient high-quality issue descriptions in real-world software datasets, which mislead automated agents and hinder their problem-solving abilities.
AI.method: The method proposed is SWE-Fuse, comprising two modules: an issue-free-driven trajectory learning module that focuses on step-by-step debugging without misleading issue descriptions, and an entropy-aware RLVR training module that adjusts training stability and exploration based on entropy levels.
AI.result: SWE-Fuse achieved a 43.0% and 60.2% improvement in solve rates compared to the best 8B and 32B baselines. Integration with test-time scaling (TTS) further enhanced solve rates to 49.8% and 65.2% for the respective models.
AI.conclusion: The introduction of SWE-Fuse significantly boosts performance in real-world software problem solving by effectively managing noisy issue descriptions, showcasing its reliability and potential in improving LLM-based agent effectiveness.

title: AutoChecklist: Composable Pipelines for Checklist Generation and Scoring with LLM-as-a-Judge
summary: Checklists have emerged as a popular approach for interpretable and fine-grained evaluation, particularly with LLM-as-a-Judge. Beyond evaluation, these structured criteria can serve as signals for model alignment, reinforcement learning, and self-correction. To support these use cases, we present AutoChecklist, an open-source library that unifies checklist-based evaluation into composable pipelines. At its core is a taxonomy of five checklist generation abstractions, each encoding a distinct strategy for deriving evaluation criteria. A modular Generator $\rightarrow$ Refiner $\rightarrow$ Scorer pipeline connects any generator with a unified scorer, and new configurations can be registered via prompt templates alone. The library ships with ten built-in pipelines implementing published approaches and supports multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, OpenRouter, vLLM). Beyond the Python API, the library includes a CLI for off-the-shelf evaluation and a web interface for interactive exploration. Validation experiments confirm that these checklist methods significantly align with human preferences and quality ratings, and a case study on ICLR peer review rebuttals demonstrates flexible domain adaptation. AutoChecklist is publicly available at https://github.com/ChicagoHAI/AutoChecklist.

AI.tldr: AutoChecklist is an open-source library offering a unified framework for checklist-based evaluation through modular pipelines that support multiple LLM providers and domains.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified and flexible tool for checklist-based evaluations that align with human preferences, supporting applications like model alignment, reinforcement learning, and domain adaptation.
AI.method: Introduces a taxonomy of checklist generation strategies and a modular pipeline (Generator → Refiner → Scorer) that adapts existing approaches using prompt templates and supports multiple Large Language Model (LLM) platforms.
AI.result: Validation experiments show checklist evaluations align with human preferences. A case study on ICLR peer review rebuttals shows effective domain adaptation.
AI.conclusion: AutoChecklist proves effective for interpretable and flexible checklist-based evaluations, supporting its use in model alignment and other applications.

title: Structural Causal Bottleneck Models
summary: We introduce structural causal bottleneck models (SCBMs), a novel class of structural causal models. At the core of SCBMs lies the assumption that causal effects between high-dimensional variables only depend on low-dimensional summary statistics, or bottlenecks, of the causes. SCBMs provide a flexible framework for task-specific dimension reduction while being estimable via standard, simple learning algorithms in practice. We analyse identifiability in SCBMs, connect them to information bottlenecks in the sense of Tishby & Zaslavsky (2015), and illustrate how to estimate them experimentally. We also demonstrate the benefit of bottlenecks for effect estimation in low-sample transfer learning settings. We argue that SCBMs provide an alternative to existing causal dimension reduction frameworks like causal representation learning or causal abstraction learning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Structural Causal Bottleneck Models (SCBMs), simplifying causal effect estimation between high-dimensional variables using low-dimensional summaries.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of causal effect estimation in high-dimensional data and improve accuracy in low-sample transfer learning settings.
AI.method: Propose SCBMs, which rely on low-dimensional causal bottlenecks, analyze identifiability, and connect them to information bottleneck theory.
AI.result: SCBMs simplify dimension reduction, are estimable with standard algorithms, and improve effect estimation in low-sample transfer learning scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SCBMs offer a flexible and effective alternative to existing causal dimension reduction approaches like causal representation and abstraction learning.

title: Collaborative Planning with Concurrent Synchronization for Operationally Constrained UAV-UGV Teams
summary: Collaborative planning under operational constraints is an essential capability for heterogeneous robot teams tackling complex large-scale real-world tasks. Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) offer rapid environmental coverage, but flight time is often limited by energy constraints, whereas Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs) have greater energy capacity to support long-duration missions, but movement is constrained by traversable terrain. Individually, neither can complete tasks such as environmental monitoring. Effective UAV-UGV collaboration therefore requires energy-constrained multi-UAV task planning, traversability-constrained multi-UGV path planning, and crucially, synchronized concurrent co-planning to ensure timely in-mission recharging. To enable these capabilities, we propose Collaborative Planning with Concurrent Synchronization (CoPCS), a learning-based approach that integrates a heterogeneous graph transformer for operationally constrained task encoding with a transformer decoder for joint, synchronized co-planning that enables UAVs and UGVs to act concurrently in a coordinated manner. CoPCS is trained end-to-end under a unified imitation learning paradigm. We conducted extensive experiments to evaluate CoPCS in both robotic simulations and physical robot teams. Experimental results demonstrate that our method provides the novel multi-robot capability of synchronized concurrent co-planning and substantially improves team performance. More details of this work are available on the project website: https://hcrlab.gitlab.io/project/CoPCS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Collaborative Planning with Concurrent Synchronization (CoPCS), a learning approach for synchronized UAV-UGV task planning to address operational constraints.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous robot teams require effective collaboration for completing large-scale tasks with operational constraints such as energy for UAVs and terrain traversability for UGVs.
AI.method: The CoPCS framework integrates a heterogeneous graph transformer for task encoding with a transformer decoder for synchronized concurrent planning, trained end-to-end using imitation learning.
AI.result: Experimental results show improvements in synchronized planning and team performance in both simulations and real-world robotics.
AI.conclusion: CoPCS enables coordinated concurrent operations for UAVs and UGVs, enhancing efficiency and addressing constraints in heterogeneous robotics.

title: Evo: Autoregressive-Diffusion Large Language Models with Evolving Balance
summary: We introduce \textbf{Evo}, a duality latent trajectory model that bridges autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based language generation within a continuous evolutionary generative framework. Rather than treating AR decoding and diffusion generation as separate paradigms, Evo reconceptualizes text generation as a latent flow: each token is associated with a vector-valued embedding that evolves over a progression variable $t_i \in [0, 1]$, indicating its semantic maturity. Low $t_i$ values correspond to confident AR-like refinement, while high values invoke diffusion-style planning, allowing the model to adaptively balance AR and diffusion based on uncertainty. Theoretically, we show that both AR and diffusion models emerge as discretizations of a shared probability flow, and we derive Evo's training objective from a unified variational ELBO. The model is implemented as a time-conditioned Transformer governed by a shared vector field, trained end-to-end to jointly infer latent codes and their progression times. During decoding, Evo performs efficient, semantics-aware refinement, achieving high-quality outputs without sacrificing speed. Empirically, Evo 8B achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive results on 15 diverse benchmarks, including reasoning (GSM8K, ARC-C), code generation (HumanEval, MBPP), and general language understanding, while maintaining fast inference speed. Our results demonstrate that Evo delivers a new paradigm for LLM design with strong generation quality, robust symbolic reasoning, and decoding efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Evo, a dual latent trajectory model combining autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based text generation paradigms for high-quality, efficient language modeling.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between AR and diffusion-based language generation, creating a model that benefits from both paradigms for improved semantic understanding and generation efficiency.
AI.method: Evo models text generation as a continuous latent flow, where tokens evolve over a progression variable spanning AR-style refinement and diffusion-style planning. This is achieved using a unified variational ELBO objective and a time-conditioned Transformer with a shared vector field.
AI.result: Evo 8B achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results across 15 benchmarks for reasoning, code generation, and language understanding while maintaining fast inference speeds.
AI.conclusion: Evo introduces a novel framework for LLMs, delivering a balance between high-quality outputs, robust reasoning, and efficient decoding by reconciling AR and diffusion-based generative approaches in a continuous trajectory.

title: Animating Petascale Time-varying Data on Commodity Hardware with LLM-assisted Scripting
comment: Will appear in ICBDA 2026. N.B. Due to the limitation "The abstract field cannot be longer than 1,920 characters", the abstract here is shorter than that in the PDF file Subjects
summary: Scientists face significant visualization challenges as time-varying datasets grow in speed and volume, often requiring specialized infrastructure and expertise to handle massive datasets. Petascale climate models generated in NASA laboratories require a dedicated group of graphics and media experts and access to high-performance computing resources. Scientists may need to share scientific results with the community iteratively and quickly. However, the time-consuming trial-and-error process incurs significant data transfer overhead and far exceeds the time and resources allocated for typical post-analysis visualization tasks, disrupting the production workflow. Our paper introduces a user-friendly framework for creating 3D animations of petascale, time-varying data on a commodity workstation. Our contributions: (i) Generalized Animation Descriptor (GAD) with a keyframe-based adaptable abstraction for animation, (ii) efficient data access from cloud-hosted repositories to reduce data management overhead, (iii) tailored rendering system, and (iv) an LLM-assisted conversational interface as a scripting module to allow domain scientists with no visualization expertise to create animations of their region of interest. We demonstrate the framework's effectiveness with two case studies: first, by generating animations in which sampling criteria are specified based on prior knowledge, and second, by generating AI-assisted animations in which sampling parameters are derived from natural-language user prompts. In all cases, we use large-scale NASA climate-oceanographic datasets that exceed 1PB in size yet achieve a fast turnaround time of 1 minute to 2 hours. Users can generate a rough draft of the animation within minutes, then seamlessly incorporate as much high-resolution data as needed for the final version.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a framework for creating 3D animations of large-scale time-varying datasets on standard workstations efficiently.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of visualizing large-scale, time-varying datasets that require significant resources, infrastructure, expertise, and quick iterative sharing of results.
AI.method: The framework introduces a Generalized Animation Descriptor (GAD), efficient cloud data access mechanisms, a tailored rendering system, and an LLM-assisted conversational interface for simplifying animation workflows.
AI.result: The framework enables the generation of animations from NASA's 1PB-plus datasets in a fast turnaround time between 1 minute to 2 hours, with effective functionality demonstrated via two case studies.
AI.conclusion: This framework facilitates user-friendly, efficient, and scalable animation creation for large scientific datasets, reducing overhead and expertise barriers for scientists while integrating AI and cloud resources.

title: AutoFigure-Edit: Generating Editable Scientific Illustration
summary: High-quality scientific illustrations are essential for communicating complex scientific and technical concepts, yet existing automated systems remain limited in editability, stylistic controllability, and efficiency. We present AutoFigure-Edit, an end-to-end system that generates fully editable scientific illustrations from long-form scientific text while enabling flexible style adaptation through user-provided reference images. By combining long-context understanding, reference-guided styling, and native SVG editing, it enables efficient creation and refinement of high-quality scientific illustrations. To facilitate further progress in this field, we release the video at https://youtu.be/10IH8SyJjAQ, full codebase at https://github.com/ResearAI/AutoFigure-Edit and provide a website for easy access and interactive use at https://deepscientist.cc/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoFigure-Edit, a tool for creating fully editable scientific illustrations from long-form text while providing style customization features.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of current automated systems in creating scientific illustrations, namely their lack of editability, stylistic control, and efficiency.
AI.method: The method involves an end-to-end system that combines long-context understanding, reference-guided styling, and native SVG editing to create editable and stylistically adaptable illustrations.
AI.result: The AutoFigure-Edit system effectively generates high-quality, editable scientific illustrations with style control features using user-provided references.
AI.conclusion: AutoFigure-Edit improves illustration creation efficiency and flexibility, filling gaps in existing automated solutions by allowing both editability and stylistic adaptation.

title: The Need for Quantitative Resilience Models and Metrics in Classical-Quantum Computing Systems
summary: Increasingly deeper integration of HPC resources and QPUs unveils new challenges in computer architecture and engineering. As a consequence, dependability arises again as a concern encompassing resilience, reproducibility and security. The properties of quantum computing systems involve a reinterpretation of these factors in retrodictive, predictive, and prescriptive ways. We state here that resilience must become an \emph{a priori} design constraint rather than an afterthought of HPC-QPU integration. This article describes the need for conceptual and quantitative models to estimate and assess the resilience hybrid classical-quantum computing infrastructure. We suggest how resilience methods in civil engineering can apply at various levels of the classical-quantum computing stack. We also discuss implications of a model of end-user value for the estimation of consequences resulting from the propagation of vulnerabilities from a given level of the stack upwards. Finally, we argue in favor of new resilience models can help the impact of improving specific components in quantum technology stacks to provide a clearer picture about the value of separation of concerns across different layers. Ultimately, HPC-QPU integration will increasingly demand more precise statements about the cost-benefit ratio of specific system improvements and their cascading consequences against estimates of delivered value to users.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies the integration challenges of HPC and QPUs, emphasizing the importance of resilience as a design constraint across computing layers.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of dependability (resilience, reproducibility, security) in hybrid classical-quantum computing infrastructures due to the deeper integration of HPC and QPUs.
AI.method: The use of conceptual and quantitative models inspired by civil engineering to estimate and assess resilience within the classical-quantum computing stack. It also proposes applying layered approaches for better separation of concerns.
AI.result: The approach provides insights on how integrating resilience methods can affect specific components of the quantum technology stack and improve user value.
AI.conclusion: New resilience models are critical to better quantify cost-benefit analyses, system improvements, and their impacts across classical-quantum computing infrastructure layers.

title: An explainable hybrid deep learning-enabled intelligent fault detection and diagnosis approach for automotive software systems validation
summary: Advancements in data-driven machine learning have emerged as a pivotal element in supporting automotive software systems (ASSs) engineering across various levels of the V-development process. Duringsystemverificationandvalidation,theintegrationofanintelligent fault detection anddiagnosis (FDD) model with test recordings analysis process serves as a powerful tool for efficiency ensuring functional safety. However, the lack of interpretability of the black-box FDD models developed not only hinders understanding of the cause underlying the prediction, but also prevents the model from being adapted based on the prediction result. This, in turn, increases the computational cost required for developingacomplexFDDmodelandlimitsconfidenceinreal-timesafety-criticalapplications.To address this challenge, a novel explainable method for fault detection, identification, and localization is proposed in this article with the aim of providing a clear understanding of the logic behind the prediction outcome. To this end, a hybrid 1dCNN-GRU-based intelligent model was developed to analyze the recordings from the real-time validation process of ASSs. The employment of explainable AI techniques, i.e., IGs, DeepLIFT, Gradient SHAP, and DeepLIFT SHAP, was instrumental in enabling model adaptation and facilitating the root cause analysis (RCA). The proposed approach is applied to the real time dataset collected during a virtual test drive performed by the user on hardware in the loop system.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel explainable AI method aimed at fault detection, identification, and localization in automotive software systems using hybrid deep learning and interpretability techniques.
AI.motivation: The lack of interpretability in black-box fault detection models hinders understanding of predictions, impedes model adaptation, and increases computational cost, limiting usage in safety-critical applications.
AI.method: The authors developed a hybrid 1D CNN-GRU-based model and employed explainable AI techniques (e.g., IGs, DeepLIFT, Gradient SHAP) to analyze test recordings and provide interpretability, enabling root cause analysis and model adaptation.
AI.result: The approach was successfully applied to a real-time dataset from a virtual test drive on a hardware-in-the-loop system, proving its efficacy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed explainable approach enhances understanding of model predictions, reduces computational costs, and improves adaptability for safety-critical applications in automotive engineering.

title: Hit-RAG: Learning to Reason with Long Contexts via Preference Alignment
summary: Despite the promise of Retrieval-Augmented Generation in grounding Multimodal Large Language Models with external knowledge, the transition to extensive contexts often leads to significant attention dilution and reasoning hallucinations. The surge in information density causes critical evidence to be submerged by voluminous noise, which complicates the discernment of relevant fragments within a dense input. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Hit-RAG}, a multi-stage preference alignment framework designed to resolve these cognitive bottlenecks through a progressive optimization pipeline. Our approach systematically refines the utilization of external evidence via three distinct stages. First, Supervised Fine-tuning establishes baseline context awareness to minimize information neglect. Next, Discriminative Preference Alignment enhances robustness against misleading distractors. Finally, Group-Relative Policy Optimization stabilizes logical synthesis to prevent reasoning collapse. Extensive evaluations on eight benchmarks demonstrate that Hit-RAG consistently yields substantial performance gains, enabling models to bridge the gap between context acquisition and accurate reasoning while surpassing much larger counterparts in long-context scenarios.

AI.tldr: Retrieval-Augmented Generation models for multimodal learning struggle with long contexts leading to reasoning errors. The authors propose Hit-RAG, a multi-stage optimization framework, to handle these challenges effectively, showing superior results across eight benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address attention dilution and reasoning hallucinations in Retrieval-Augmented Generation models handling extensive, dense contexts.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: 1) Supervised Fine-tuning for baseline context awareness, 2) Discriminative Preference Alignment to manage noise, and 3) Group-Relative Policy Optimization for logical consistency.
AI.result: Hit-RAG demonstrated substantial performance improvements, outperforming larger models in handling long-context scenarios during evaluations on eight benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hit-RAG successfully enhances the grounding and reasoning capabilities of multimodal models, overcoming the bottlenecks of large and dense input contexts.

title: SysNav: Multi-Level Systematic Cooperation Enables Real-World, Cross-Embodiment Object Navigation
summary: Object navigation (ObjectNav) in real-world environments is a complex problem that requires simultaneously addressing multiple challenges, including complex spatial structure, long-horizon planning and semantic understanding.
