AI.method: The authors propose a framework leveraging the Generalized Fourier Transform (GFT) to detect symmetries in spectral sparsity patterns rather than optimizing over generators.
AI.result: The proposed method identifies symmetries across various tasks, such as double pendulum dynamics and top quark tagging, proving its reliability and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Spectral sparsity analysis provides a principled and interpretable way to discover continuous one-parameter symmetries, presenting an alternative approach to traditional methods.

title: Prompt-Based Caption Generation for Single-Tooth Dental Images Using Vision-Language Models
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Semantic Computing (IEEE ICSC 2026)
summary: Digital dentistry has made significant advances with the advent of deep learning. However, the majority of these deep learning-based dental image analysis models focus on very specific tasks such as tooth segmentation, tooth detection, cavity detection, and gingivitis classification. There is a lack of a specialized model that has holistic knowledge of teeth and can perform dental image analysis tasks based on that knowledge. Datasets of dental images with captions can help build such a model. To the best of our knowledge, existing dental image datasets with captions are few in number and limited in scope. In many of these datasets, the captions describe the entire mouth, while the images are limited to the anterior view. As a result, posterior teeth such as molars are not clearly visible, limiting the usefulness of the captions for training vision-language models. Additionally, the captions focus only on a specific disease (gingivitis) and do not provide a holistic assessment of each tooth. Moreover, tooth disease scores are typically assigned to individual teeth, and each tooth is treated as a separate entity in orthodontic procedures. Therefore, it is important to have captions for single-tooth images. As far as we know, no such dataset of single-tooth images with dental captions exists. In this work, we aim to bridge that gap by assessing the possibility of generating captions for dental images using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and evaluating the extent and quality of those captions. Our findings suggest that guided prompts help VLMs generate meaningful captions. We show that the prompts generated by our framework are better anchored in describing the visual aspects of dental images. We selected RGB images as they have greater potential in consumer scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the creation of a framework to generate meaningful dental image captions using Vision-Language Models (VLMs), addressing current dataset limitations.
AI.motivation: There are no specialized models with holistic knowledge of teeth leveraging captions. Existing dental image datasets are limited to anterior views or specific diseases, and no datasets focus on single-tooth images with captions.
AI.method: The authors assessed the generation of dental image captions using VLMs with guided prompts to improve the quality and relevance of the captions.
AI.result: Their approach demonstrated that guided prompts enhance the meaningfulness of captions for dental images, specifically tailoring captions relevant to individual teeth and their visual details.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the potential for VLMs with guided prompts to develop better dental imaging datasets, advancing the scope of digital dentistry with meaningful captions and addressing key dataset gaps.

title: Multifingered force-aware control for humanoid robots
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in ICRA 2026
summary: In this paper, we address force-aware control and force distribution in robotic platforms with multi-fingered hands. Given a target goal and force estimates from tactile sensors, we design a controller that adapts the motion of the torso, arm, wrist, and fingers, redistributing forces to maintain stable contact with objects of varying mass distribution or unstable contacts. To estimate forces, we collect a dataset of tactile signals and ground-truth force measurements using five Xela magnetic sensors interacting with indenters, and train force estimators. We then introduce a model-based control scheme that minimizes the distance between the Center of Pressure (CoP) and the centroid of the fingertips contact polygon. Since our method relies on estimated forces rather than raw tactile signals, it has the potential to be applied to any sensor capable of force estimation. We validate our framework on a balancing task with five objects, achieving a $82.7\%$ success rate, and further evaluate it in multi-object scenarios, achieving $80\%$ accuracy. Code and data can be found here https://github.com/hsp-iit/multifingered-force-aware-control.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a force-aware control mechanism for robotic platforms with multi-fingered hands, using force estimates from tactile sensors to adjust movement and ensure stability.
AI.motivation: The aim is to enhance robotic multi-fingered hands by enabling them to maintain stable interaction with objects of different mass distributions or unstable contacts, which broadens their usability in real-world tasks.
AI.method: A dataset of tactile signals and ground force measurements was collected using magnetic sensors. These were used to train force estimators. A model-based control scheme was implemented to minimize the distance between the Center of Pressure (CoP) and the contact polygon's centroid.
AI.result: The proposed solution achieved an 82.7% success rate for a balancing task with five objects and 80% accuracy in multi-object scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates robustness in force distribution and stability across tasks, suggesting it can be adapted to various force-estimating sensors for broader application.

title: AutoResearch-RL: Perpetual Self-Evaluating Reinforcement Learning Agents for Autonomous Neural Architecture Discovery
summary:
We present AutoResearch-RL, a framework in which a reinforcement learning agent conducts open-ended neural architecture and hyperparameter research without human supervision, running perpetually until a termination oracle signals convergence or resource exhaustion. At each step the agent proposes a code modification to a target training script, executes it under a fixed wall clock time budget, observes a scalar reward derived from validation bits-per-byte (val-bpb), and updates its policy via Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO).
  The key design insight is the separation of three concerns: (i) a frozen environment (data pipeline, evaluation protocol, and constants) that guarantees fair cross-experiment comparison; (ii) a mutable target file (train.py) that represents the agent's editable state; and (iii) a meta-learner (the RL agent itself) that accumulates a growing trajectory of experiment outcomes and uses them to inform subsequent proposals.
  We formalise this as a Markov Decision Process, derive convergence guarantees under mild assumptions, and demonstrate empirically on a single GPU nanochat pretraining benchmark that AutoResearch-RL discovers configurations that match or exceed hand-tuned baselines after approximately 300 overnight iterations, with no human in the loop.

AI.tldr: AutoResearch-RL, using reinforcement learning, autonomously performs open-ended neural architecture and hyperparameter research, achieving competitive results without human supervision.
AI.motivation: To develop a framework capable of autonomously performing neural architecture and hyperparameter tuning without requiring human intervention.
AI.method: The framework uses reinforcement learning with Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO), allowing an agent to make code modifications, execute experiments, and learn from validation rewards.
AI.result: AutoResearch-RL achieves configurations comparable to or better than hand-tuned baselines after 300 iterations on a benchmark, using only a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: AutoResearch-RL shows promise in automating neural architecture research, reducing human input and resource dependency during experiments.

title: UnSCAR: Universal, Scalable, Controllable, and Adaptable Image Restoration
summary: Universal image restoration aims to recover clean images from arbitrary real-world degradations using a single inference model. Despite significant progress, existing all-in-one restoration networks do not scale to multiple degradations. As the number of degradations increases, training becomes unstable, models grow excessively large, and performance drops across both seen and unseen domains. In this work, we show that scaling universal restoration is fundamentally limited by interference across degradations during joint learning, leading to catastrophic task forgetting. To address this challenge, we introduce a unified inference pipeline with a multi-branch mixture-of-experts architecture that decomposes restoration knowledge across specialized task-adaptable experts. Our approach enables scalable learning (over sixteen degradations), adapts and generalizes robustly to unseen domains, and supports user-controllable restoration across degradations. Beyond achieving superior performance across benchmarks, this work establishes a new design paradigm for scalable and controllable universal image restoration.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a scalable and controllable universal image restoration model that addresses interference and instability during training by using a mixture-of-experts architecture, achieving superior performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing universal image restoration models face challenges in scalability, stability, and performance drop as the number of degradations increases due to interference across degradations during joint learning.
AI.method: The paper proposes a unified inference pipeline leveraging a multi-branch mixture-of-experts architecture, which decomposes restoration knowledge into specialized task-adaptable experts.
AI.result: The new approach enables scalable learning across sixteen degradations, adapts robustly to unseen domains, supports user-controllable restoration, and achieves superior performance in benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces a novel design paradigm for universal image restoration, addressing scalability and performance issues while enhancing adaptability and control.

title: A General Lie-Group Framework for Continuum Soft Robot Modeling
summary: This paper introduces a general Lie group framework for modeling continuum soft robots, employing Cosserat rod theory combined with cumulative parameterization on the Lie group SE(3). This novel approach addresses limitations present in current strain-based and configuration-based methods by providing geometric local control and eliminating unit quaternion constraints. The paper derives unified analytical expressions for kinematics, statics, and dynamics, including recursive Jacobian computations and an energy-conserving integrator suitable for real-time simulation and control. Additionally, the framework is extended to handle complex robotic structures, including segmented, branched, nested, and rigid-soft composite configurations, facilitating a modular and unified modeling strategy. The effectiveness, generality, and computational efficiency of the proposed methodology are demonstrated through various scenarios, including large-deformation rods, concentric tube robots, parallel robots, cable-driven robots, and articulated fingers. This work enhances modeling flexibility and numerical performance, providing an improved toolset for designing, simulating, and controlling soft robotic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Lie group framework for modeling soft robots using Cosserat rod theory on SE(3), offering flexible and efficient simulation and control for complex robotic structures.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of existing strain-based and configuration-based methods in soft robotic modeling, particularly issues with unit quaternion constraints and lack of geometric local control.
AI.method: The paper develops a Lie group-based modeling framework for continuum soft robots by combining Cosserat rod theory with SE(3) cumulative parameterization. It includes unified analytical expressions for kinematics, statics, and dynamics, as well as methods like recursive Jacobians and energy-conserving integrators for real-time applications.
AI.result: The method is applied successfully to various robotic scenarios, demonstrating its effectiveness in handling large deformations, complex structures, and assorted designs with improved computational efficiency and flexibility.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a robust, modular, and unified approach for modeling, simulating, and controlling both standard and complex soft robotic systems, advancing the field significantly.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Multi-scale Framework for County-Level Crop Yield Prediction Across Large Regions
summary: This paper proposes a new method for crop yield prediction, which is essential for developing management strategies, informing insurance assessments, and ensuring long-term food security. Although existing data-driven approaches have shown promise in this domain, their performance often degrades when applied across large geographic regions and long time periods. This limitation arises from two key challenges: (1) difficulty in jointly capturing short-term and long-term temporal patterns, and (2) inability to effectively accommodate spatial data variability in agricultural systems. Ignoring these issues often leads to unreliable predictions for specific regions or years, which ultimately affects policy decisions and resource allocation. In this paper, we propose a new predictive framework to address these challenges. First, we introduce a new backbone model architecture that captures both short-term daily-scale crop growth dynamics and long-term dependencies across years. To further improve generalization across diverse spatial regions, we augment this model with a retrieval-based adaptation strategy. Recognizing the substantial yield variation across years, we design a novel retrieval-and-refinement pipeline that adjusts retrieved samples by removing cross-year bias not explained by input features. Our experiments on real-world county-level corn yield data over 630 counties in the US demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms different types of baselines. The results also verify the effectiveness of the retrieval-based augmentation method in improving model robustness under spatial heterogeneity.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to improve crop yield prediction by addressing challenges in capturing temporal patterns and spatial variability, achieving better results in heterogeneous regions.
AI.motivation: To improve crop yield prediction reliability across large geographic areas and time periods by addressing limitations in current methods related to long-term/short-term temporal patterns and spatial data variability.
AI.method: The paper presents a new model architecture for capturing both short-term and long-term crop dynamics, supplemented by a retrieval-based adaptation strategy to address spatial heterogeneity. It also incorporates a retrieval-and-refinement pipeline to adjust cross-year biases.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrated superior performance over baselines in experiments on real-world county-level corn yield data across 630 US counties. It improved robustness in accounting for spatial variability.
AI.conclusion: The study's framework effectively enhances prediction accuracy and robustness under spatial and temporal variability, showcasing its potential applications for agricultural policy and resource allocation.

title: Photons = Tokens: The Physics of AI and the Economics of Knowledge
summary: Debates about artificial intelligence capabilities and risks are often conducted without quantitative grounding. This paper applies the methodology of MacKay (2009) -- who reframed energy policy as arithmetic -- to the economy of AI computation. We define the token, the elementary unit of large language model input and output, as a physical quantity with measurable thermodynamic cost. Using Landauer's principle, Shannon's channel capacity, and current infrastructure data, we construct a supply-and-demand balance sheet for global token production. We then derive a finite question budget: the number of meaningful queries humanity can direct at AI systems under physical, information-theoretic, and economic constraints. We apply Coase's theory of the firm and the durable-goods monopoly problem to the AI value chain -- from photon to atom to chip to power to token to question -- to identify where economic value concentrates and where regulatory intervention is warranted. We argue that the expansion of the token budget does not resolve a deeper constraint: under structural uncertainty, the decisive variable is not how many questions can be answered but which questions are worth asking -- a problem of agency and direction that computation alone cannot solve. We connect limits of measurement in the token economy to a structural parallel between Goodhart's law and the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, and to Arrow's impossibility result for efficient information pricing. The framework yields order-of-magnitude estimates that discipline policy discussion: at current efficiency, the projected 2028 US AI energy allocation of 326~TWh could support roughly $6.5 \times 10^{17}$ tokens per year, or 225,000 tokens per person per day -- more than three orders of magnitude above estimated mid-2024 utilization.

AI.tldr: The paper quantifies AI computational demands, defining tokens as measurable physical units and identifying limitations in question-asking to guide AI's effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To provide a quantitative foundation for debates surrounding AI capabilities and constraints, addressing energy, economic, and thermodynamic limits.
AI.method: Uses Landauer's principle, Shannon's channel capacity, and economic theories (Coase's, durable-goods monopoly) to quantify AI computation costs and regulatory implications.
AI.result: Estimates the annual global token production achievable with projected 2028 energy allocation, finding it vastly exceeds current utilization but identifying deeper agency-related constraints.
AI.conclusion: AI's computation and token capability are physically and economically abundant, but real constraints lie in determining meaningful and valuable questions within the token budget.

title: QdaVPR: A novel query-based domain-agnostic model for visual place recognition
summary: Visual place recognition (VPR) aiming at predicting the location of an image based solely on its visual features is a fundamental task in robotics and autonomous systems. Domain variation remains one of the main challenges in VPR and is relatively unexplored. Existing VPR models attempt to achieve domain agnosticism either by training on large-scale datasets that inherently contain some domain variations, or by being specifically adapted to particular target domains. In practice, the former lacks explicit domain supervision, while the latter generalizes poorly to unseen domain shifts. This paper proposes a novel query-based domain-agnostic VPR model called QdaVPR. First, a dual-level adversarial learning framework is designed to encourage domain invariance for both the query features forming the global descriptor and the image features from which these query features are derived. Then, a triplet supervision based on query combinations is designed to enhance the discriminative power of the global descriptors. To support the learning process, we augment a large-scale VPR dataset using style transfer methods, generating various synthetic domains with corresponding domain labels as auxiliary supervision. Extensive experiments show that QdaVPR achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple VPR benchmarks with significant domain variations. Specifically, it attains the best Recall@1 and Recall@10 on nearly all test scenarios: 93.5%/98.6% on Nordland (seasonal changes), 97.5%/99.0% on Tokyo24/7 (day-night transitions), and the highest Recall@1 across almost all weather conditions on the SVOX dataset. Our code will be released at https://github.com/shuimushan/QdaVPR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces QdaVPR, a novel domain-agnostic model for visual place recognition (VPR), achieving state-of-the-art results by tackling domain shifts using dual-level adversarial learning and domain-enhanced datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of domain variation in visual place recognition, which is underexplored and problematic in generalizing across unseen domain shifts.
AI.method: Proposes QdaVPR with dual-level adversarial learning, triplet supervision for query combinations, and style transfer-augmented datasets for improved domain invariance and global descriptor discrimination.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on VPR benchmarks under domain variations such as seasons, day-night transitions, and weather conditions, achieving high recall rates.
AI.conclusion: QdaVPR effectively addresses domain variations in VPR, improving generalization and accuracy. Its release will contribute to broader adoption and further research.

title: FlowTouch: View-Invariant Visuo-Tactile Prediction
summary: Tactile sensation is essential for contact-rich manipulation tasks. It provides direct feedback on object geometry, surface properties, and interaction forces, enhancing perception and enabling fine-grained control. An inherent limitation of tactile sensors is that readings are available only when an object is touched. This precludes their use during planning and the initial execution phase of a task. Predicting tactile information from visual information can bridge this gap. A common approach is to learn a direct mapping from camera images to the output of vision-based tactile sensors. However, the resulting model will depend strongly on the specific setup and on how well the camera can capture the area where an object is touched. In this work, we introduce FlowTouch, a novel model for view-invariant visuo-tactile prediction. Our key idea is to use an object's local 3D mesh to encode rich information for predicting tactile patterns while abstracting away from scene-dependent details. FlowTouch integrates scene reconstruction and Flow Matching-based models for image generation. Our results show that FlowTouch is able to bridge the sim-to-real gap and generalize to new sensor instances. We further show that the resulting tactile images can be used for downstream grasp stability prediction. Our code, datasets and videos are available at https://flowtouch.github.io/

AI.tldr: FlowTouch is a novel model that predicts tactile sensations from visual data using 3D mesh reconstructions, allowing generalization to new sensor instances and application in grasp stability prediction.
AI.motivation: Tactile sensors are limited as they only sense information upon contact, restricting their use during planning and task initiation. Bridging tactile and visual information offers a solution.
AI.method: FlowTouch uses a local 3D mesh of objects for view-invariant visuo-tactile prediction. It integrates scene reconstruction with Flow Matching-based models for generating tactile patterns.
AI.result: FlowTouch successfully generalizes across simulation and real-world scenarios, as well as to new sensor instances, and its tactile predictions assist in grasp stability tasks.
AI.conclusion: FlowTouch enhances visuo-tactile prediction by effectively abstracting away scene-dependent details, providing valuable tools for manipulation tasks and bridging the gap between simulation and reality.

title: Disentangled Textual Priors for Diffusion-based Image Super-Resolution
summary: Image Super-Resolution (SR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution images from degraded low-resolution inputs. While diffusion-based SR methods offer powerful generative capabilities, their performance heavily depends on how semantic priors are structured and integrated into the generation process. Existing approaches often rely on entangled or coarse-grained priors that mix global layout with local details, or conflate structural and textural cues, thereby limiting semantic controllability and interpretability. In this work, we propose DTPSR, a novel diffusion-based SR framework that introduces disentangled textual priors along two complementary dimensions: spatial hierarchy (global vs. local) and frequency semantics (low- vs. high-frequency). By explicitly separating these priors, DTPSR enables the model to simultaneously capture scene-level structure and object-specific details with frequency-aware semantic guidance. The corresponding embeddings are injected via specialized cross-attention modules, forming a progressive generation pipeline that reflects the semantic granularity of visual content, from global layout to fine-grained textures. To support this paradigm, we construct DisText-SR, a large-scale dataset containing approximately 95,000 image-text pairs with carefully disentangled global, low-frequency, and high-frequency descriptions. To further enhance controllability and consistency, we adopt a multi-branch classifier-free guidance strategy with frequency-aware negative prompts to suppress hallucinations and semantic drift. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that DTPSR achieves high perceptual quality, competitive fidelity, and strong generalization across diverse degradation scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DTPSR, a diffusion-based super-resolution framework that uses disentangled semantic priors for capturing global structure and fine details in images.
AI.motivation: Improve semantic controllability and interpretability in current diffusion-based super-resolution methods, which struggle with mixing or conflating global and local semantic dimensions.
AI.method: Propose DTPSR that separates textual priors into spatial hierarchy (global vs. local) and frequency semantics (low- vs. high-frequency). Employ cross-attention modules and progressive generation pipelines alongside a curated dataset and classifier-free guidance strategy.
AI.result: DTPSR demonstrates high-quality image generation, improved consistency, and generalization across synthetic and real-world benchmarks with diverse degradations.
AI.conclusion: DTPSR offers an effective solution for disentangling semantic priors, enhancing SR interpretability, controllability, and achieving competitive fidelity and perceptual quality.

title: Generalization in Online Reinforcement Learning for Mobile Agents
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI)-based mobile agents automate digital tasks on mobile devices by interpreting natural-language instructions and interacting with the screen. While recent methods apply reinforcement learning (RL) to train vision-language-model(VLM) agents in interactive environments with a primary focus on performance, generalization remains underexplored due to the lack of standardized benchmarks and open-source RL systems. In this work, we formalize the problem as a Contextual Markov Decision Process (CMDP) and introduce \textbf{AndroidWorld-Generalization}, a benchmark with three increasingly challenging regimes for evaluating zero-shot generalization to unseen task instances, templates, and applications. We further propose an RL training system that integrates Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a scalable rollout collection system, consisting of containerized infrastructure and asynchronous execution % , and error recovery to support reliable and efficient training. Experiments on AndroidWorld-Generalization show that RL enables a 7B-parameter VLM agent to surpass supervised fine-tuning baselines, yielding a 26.1\% improvement on unseen instances but only limited gains on unseen templates (15.7\%) and apps (8.3\%), underscoring the challenges of generalization. As a preliminary step, we demonstrate that few-shot adaptation at test-time improves performance on unseen apps, motivating future research in this direction. To support reproducibility and fair comparison, we open-source the full RL training system, including the environment, task suite, models, prompt configurations, and the underlying infrastructure \footnote{https://github.com/zihuanjiang/AndroidWorld-Generalization}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AndroidWorld-Generalization, a benchmark for evaluating generalization in RL-trained GUI-based mobile agents and proposes a scalable RL system for enhancements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses issues in generalization for GUI-based mobile agents trained with RL, focusing on building standardized benchmarks and systems to improve generalization beyond specific templates and applications.
AI.method: The research formalizes generalization in RL as a Contextual Markov Decision Process and proposes AndroidWorld-Generalization while introducing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with scalable infrastructure.
AI.result: Using AndroidWorld-Generalization, experiments demonstrate RL-trained VLM agents outperform supervised baselines on unseen tasks but have limited generalization ability for unseen templates and apps. Few-shot adaptation showed promise.
AI.conclusion: This study highlights the challenges in agent generalization, underlining the necessity for better mechanisms like few-shot adaptation to address unseen scenarios, and provides open-source tools to assist future research.

title: Seed2Scale: A Self-Evolving Data Engine for Embodied AI via Small to Large Model Synergy and Multimodal Evaluation
summary: Existing data generation methods suffer from exploration limits, embodiment gaps, and low signal-to-noise ratios, leading to performance degradation during self-iteration. To address these challenges, we propose Seed2Scale, a self-evolving data engine that overcomes the data bottleneck through a heterogeneous synergy of "small-model collection, large-model evaluation, and target-model learning". Starting with as few as four seed demonstrations, the engine employs the lightweight Vision-Language-Action model, SuperTiny, as a dedicated collector, leveraging its strong inductive bias for robust exploration in parallel environments. Concurrently, a pre-trained Vision-Language Model is integrated as a Verifer to autonomously perform success/failure judgment and quality scoring for the massive generated trajectories. Seed2Scale effectively mitigates model collapse, ensuring the stability of the self-evolution process. Experimental results demonstrate that Seed2Scale exhibits signifcant scaling potential: as iterations progress, the success rate of the target model shows a robust upward trend, achieving a performance improvement of 131.2%. Furthermore, Seed2Scale signifcantly outperforms existing data augmentation methods, providing a scalable and cost-effective pathway for the large-scale development of Generalist Embodied AI. Project page: https://terminators2025.github.io/Seed2Scale.github.io

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Seed2Scale, a self-evolving data engine addressing exploration limits, embodiment gaps, and signal-to-noise challenges for large-scale Generalist Embodied AI development.
AI.motivation: Existing data generation methods face exploration limitations, embodiment gaps, and noise issues that hinder self-iteration performance.
AI.method: Seed2Scale combines small-model collections, large-model evaluations, and target-model learning, using a lightweight model for exploration and a pre-trained verifier for quality assessment.
AI.result: Seed2Scale achieves a 131.2% performance improvement, ensuring stable self-evolution and surpassing existing data augmentation methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers an efficient, scalable solution for advancing Generalist Embodied AI by overcoming data bottlenecks and enhancing performance.

title: ShakyPrepend: A Multi-Group Learner with Improved Sample Complexity
summary: Multi-group learning is a learning task that focuses on controlling predictors' conditional losses over specified subgroups. We propose ShakyPrepend, a method that leverages tools inspired by differential privacy to obtain improved theoretical guarantees over existing approaches. Through numerical experiments, we demonstrate that ShakyPrepend adapts to both group structure and spatial heterogeneity. We provide practical guidance for deploying multi-group learning algorithms in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ShakyPrepend, a novel method for multi-group learning using differential privacy techniques and offers theoretical and numerical evidence of its advantages.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of controlling predictors' conditional losses across specified subgroups in multi-group learning.
AI.method: ShakyPrepend utilizes tools inspired by differential privacy to improve theoretical guarantees and adapt to group structures and spatial heterogeneity.
AI.result: The method demonstrated adaptability through numerical experiments and provided improved theoretical guarantees compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ShakyPrepend can effectively enhance multi-group learning tasks and offers practical guidance for real-world application.

title: Dial: A Knowledge-Grounded Dialect-Specific NL2SQL System
summary:
Enterprises commonly deploy heterogeneous database systems, each of which owns a distinct SQL dialect with different syntax rules, built-in functions, and execution constraints. However, most existing NL2SQL methods assume a single dialect (e.g., SQLite) and struggle to produce queries that are both semantically correct and executable on target engines. Prompt-based approaches tightly couple intent reasoning with dialect syntax, rule-based translators often degrade native operators into generic constructs, and multi-dialect fine-tuning suffers from cross-dialect interference.
  In this paper, we present Dial, a knowledge-grounded framework for dialect-specific NL2SQL. Dial introduces: (1) a Dialect-Aware Logical Query Planning module that converts natural language into a dialect-aware logical query plan via operator-level intent decomposition and divergence-aware specification; (2) HINT-KB, a hierarchical intent-aware knowledge base that organizes dialect knowledge into (i) a canonical syntax reference, (ii) a declarative function repository, and (iii) a procedural constraint repository; and (3) an execution-driven debugging and semantic verification loop that separates syntactic recovery from logic auditing to prevent semantic drift. We construct DS-NL2SQL, a benchmark covering six major database systems with 2,218 dialect-specific test cases. Experimental results show that Dial consistently improves translation accuracy by 10.25% and dialect feature coverage by 15.77% over state-of-the-art baselines. The code is at https://github.com/weAIDB/Dial.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'Dial', a framework for translating natural language to dialect-specific SQL queries, addressing challenges in multi-dialect translation with innovative modules and achieving significant improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing NL2SQL methods fail to address the complexities of handling multiple SQL dialects with varied syntax and constraints, creating challenges for enterprise-level heterogeneous database systems.
AI.method: The Dial framework comprises three core components: Dialect-Aware Logical Query Planning, HINT-KB (a knowledge base for dialect syntax and constraints), and an execution-driven debugging loop for query verification.
AI.result: Dial showed a 10.25% improvement in translation accuracy and a 15.77% enhancement in dialect feature coverage compared to leading approaches on the newly introduced DS-NL2SQL benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Dial efficiently enhances NL2SQL capabilities for multiple SQL dialects through knowledge-grounded strategies, resolving issues in dialect-specific query generation and verification while setting new benchmarks for the domain.

title: SAIL: Test-Time Scaling for In-Context Imitation Learning with VLM
summary: In-context imitation learning allows robots to acquire skills from demonstrations, yet one-shot trajectory generation remains fragile under environmental variation. We propose SAIL, a framework that reframes robot imitation as an iterative refinement problem capable of scaling with test-time compute. SAIL utilizes Monte Carlo Tree Search, where each node is a complete trajectory and edges correspond to trajectory refinements. The process is guided by three core components: an automated archive of successful trajectories for contextually relevant retrieval, a vision language model-based scoring mechanism for trajectory evaluation, and a step-level feedback that provides trajectory-aligned scores for iterative refinement. Experiments across six diverse manipulation tasks in simulation and real-world validation clearly demonstrate that increasing test-time compute consistently improves success rates, achieving up to 95% on complex tasks. Our results suggest that trajectory-level test-time scaling is a robust path toward more generalizable robotic agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SAIL, a framework that enhances robot imitation learning by iteratively refining trajectories using Monte Carlo Tree Search, achieving improved success rates in manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Current in-context imitation learning methods struggle with one-shot trajectory generation under environmental variance, necessitating more robust and scalable approaches.
AI.method: Utilizes Monte Carlo Tree Search with nodes representing complete trajectories and edges as trajectory refinements, guided by an archive for relevant retrieval, a vision language model-based evaluation mechanism, and step-level feedback for iterative refinement.
AI.result: The method was tested in six diverse manipulation tasks in both simulation and real-world settings. It showed consistent success rate improvement with increased test-time compute, achieving up to 95% success on complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that trajectory-level test-time scaling is a viable and effective strategy for creating more generalizable robotic skills across diverse contexts.

title: Norm-Hierarchy Transitions in Representation Learning: When and Why Neural Networks Abandon Shortcuts
summary:
Neural networks often rely on spurious shortcuts for many epochs before discovering structured representations. However, the mechanism governing when this transition occurs and whether its timing can be predicted remains unclear. Prior work shows that gradient descent converges to low norm solutions and that neural networks exhibit simplicity bias, but neither explains the timescale of the transition from shortcut features to structured representations.
  We introduce the Norm-Hierarchy Transition (NHT) framework, which explains delayed representation learning as the slow traversal of a hierarchy of parameter norms during regularized optimization. When multiple interpolating solutions exist with different norms, weight decay gradually moves the model from high norm shortcut solutions toward lower norm structured representations. We derive a tight bound showing that the transition delay grows logarithmically with the ratio between shortcut and structured norms.
  Experiments on modular arithmetic, CIFAR-10 with spurious features, CelebA, and Waterbirds support the predictions of the framework. The results suggest that grokking, shortcut learning, and delayed feature discovery arise from a common mechanism based on norm hierarchy traversal during training.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Norm-Hierarchy Transition (NHT) framework to explain why neural networks rely on shortcuts before learning structured representations, linking it to slow learning dynamics influenced by parameter norms.
AI.motivation: To understand and predict the timing of transitions in neural networks from using spurious shortcuts to discovering structured representations during training.
AI.method: Introduced the Norm-Hierarchy Transition (NHT) framework, theorizing that regularized optimization gradually shifts models from high norm solutions (shortcuts) to lower norm structured representations. A mathematical bound is proposed to relate this delay to the norm ratio.
AI.result: Empirical validation was conducted on tasks like modular arithmetic, CIFAR-10 with spurious features, CelebA, and Waterbirds. Results confirm the framework's predictions about delayed representation learning.
AI.conclusion: The NHT framework suggests that phenomena like grokking and delayed feature discovery are caused by norm hierarchy traversal during training, offering new insights into learning dynamics.

title: RPG-SAM: Reliability-Weighted Prototypes and Geometric Adaptive Threshold Selection for Training-Free One-Shot Polyp Segmentation
comment: Under review at MICCAI 2026. 8 pages, 3 figures
summary: Training-free one-shot segmentation offers a scalable alternative to expert annotations where knowledge is often transferred from support images and foundation models. But existing methods often treat all pixels in support images and query response intensities models in a homogeneous way. They ignore the regional heterogeity in support images and response heterogeity in query.To resolve this, we propose RPG-SAM, a framework that systematically tackles these heterogeneity gaps. Specifically, to address regional heterogeneity, we introduce Reliability-Weighted Prototype Mining (RWPM) to prioritize high-fidelity support features while utilizing background anchors as contrastive references for noise suppression. To address response heterogeneity, we develop Geometric Adaptive Selection (GAS) to dynamically recalibrate binarization thresholds by evaluating the morphological consensus of candidates. Finally, an iterative refinement loop method is designed to polishes anatomical boundaries. By accounting for multi-layered information heterogeneity, RPG-SAM achieves a 5.56\% mIoU improvement on the Kvasir dataset. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes RPG-SAM, a training-free one-shot segmentation method that systematically overcomes heterogeneity issues in support image features and foundation model responses, improving segmentation results.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of current one-shot segmentation methods that ignore regional and response heterogeneity in support images and model responses.
AI.method: Introduced three main components: (1) Reliability-Weighted Prototype Mining (RWPM) to emphasize high-fidelity support features with noise suppression, (2) Geometric Adaptive Selection (GAS) for dynamic binarization threshold recalibration, and (3) an iterative refinement loop for precise anatomical boundaries.
AI.result: RPG-SAM demonstrates significant performance improvement, achieving a 5.56% mIoU increase on the Kvasir dataset.
AI.conclusion: By addressing multi-layered heterogeneity in data and model responses, RPG-SAM offers a more effective approach to training-free one-shot segmentation. The code will be made available for replication and further development.

title: Image Generation Models: A Technical History
summary: Image generation has advanced rapidly over the past decade, yet the literature seems fragmented across different models and application domains. This paper aims to offer a comprehensive survey of breakthrough image generation models, including variational autoencoders (VAEs), generative adversarial networks (GANs), normalizing flows, autoregressive and transformer-based generators, and diffusion-based methods. We provide a detailed technical walkthrough of each model type, including their underlying objectives, architectural building blocks, and algorithmic training steps. For each model type, we present the optimization techniques as well as common failure modes and limitations. We also go over recent developments in video generation and present the research works that made it possible to go from still frames to high quality videos. Lastly, we cover the growing importance of robustness and responsible deployment of these models, including deepfake risks, detection, artifacts, and watermarking.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys advancements in image generation models, covering types like VAEs, GANs, transformers, and diffusion-based methods. It addresses technical details, applications, limitations, and ethical concerns.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to consolidate the fragmented literature and provide a comprehensive understanding of key advancements and challenges in image generation.
AI.method: A technical walkthrough is presented for each type of image generation model, analyzing objectives, architectures, training steps, optimization techniques, and failure modes.
AI.result: The paper identifies advancements in video generation, alongside increasing focus on robustness and responsible deployment of image generation models.
AI.conclusion: With detailed survey and analysis, the paper bridges gaps in understanding image generation technologies, emphasizing their development, limitations, and ethical ramifications.

title: Less is More: Robust Zero-Communication 3D Pursuit-Evasion via Representational Parsimony
comment: 7 pages, 10 figures. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Asymmetric 3D pursuit-evasion in cluttered voxel environments is difficult under communication latency, partial observability, and nonholonomic maneuver limits. While many MARL methods rely on richer inter-agent coupling or centralized signals, these dependencies can become fragility sources when communication is delayed or noisy. Building on an inherited path-guided decentralized pursuit scaffold, we study a robustness-oriented question: can representational parsimony improve communication-free coordination? We instantiate this principle with (i) a parsimonious actor observation interface that removes team-coupled channels (83-D to 50-D), and (ii) Contribution-Gated Credit Assignment (CGCA), a locality-aware credit structure for communication-denied cooperation. In Stage-5 evaluation (4 pursuers vs. 1 evader), our configuration reaches 0.753 +/- 0.091 success and 0.223 +/- 0.066 collision, outperforming the 83-D FULL OBS counterpart (0.721 +/- 0.071, 0.253 +/- 0.089). It further shows graceful degradation under speed/yaw/noise/delay stress tests and resilient zero-shot transfer on urban-canyon maps (about 61% success at density 0.24). These results support a practical paradigm shift: explicitly severing redundant cross-agent channels can suppress compounding error cascades and improve robustness in latency-prone deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper explores robustness in 3D pursuit-evasion scenarios, developing methods to enhance decentralized communication-free coordination and demonstrating superior results in both performance metrics and stress scenarios compared to conventional approaches.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of pursuit-evasion tasks in constrained environments, specifically under conditions of latency, limited communication, and maneuverability constraints. The authors aim to reduce reliance on high inter-agent coupling and centralized signals, which are prone to fragility.
AI.method: The authors propose a minimally connected observation interface, reducing observation dimensions and removing centralized signals in a MARL setting. They introduce Contribution-Gated Credit Assignment (CGCA) to aid coordination in communication-denied environments.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieved better success rates (0.753) and lower collision rates (0.223) compared to a richer observation counterpart. It demonstrated robustness to stress tests involving environmental and behavioral uncertainties and successful generalization to new urban environments.
AI.conclusion: By reducing redundant inter-agent dependencies and simplifying communication structures, the proposed methods enhance robustness and coordination in latency-sensitive and partially observable environments.

title: Learning Concept Bottleneck Models from Mechanistic Explanations
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) aim for ante-hoc interpretability by learning a bottleneck layer that predicts interpretable concepts before the decision. State-of-the-art approaches typically select which concepts to learn via human specification, open knowledge graphs, prompting an LLM, or using general CLIP concepts. However, concepts defined a-priori may not have sufficient predictive power for the task or even be learnable from the available data. As a result, these CBMs often significantly trail their black-box counterpart when controlling for information leakage. To address this, we introduce a novel CBM pipeline named Mechanistic CBM (M-CBM), which builds the bottleneck directly from a black-box model's own learned concepts. These concepts are extracted via Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) and subsequently named and annotated on a selected subset of images using a Multimodal LLM. For fair comparison and leakage control, we also introduce the Number of Contributing Concepts (NCC), a decision-level sparsity metric that extends the recently proposed NEC metric. Across diverse datasets, we show that M-CBMs consistently surpass prior CBMs at matched sparsity, while improving concept predictions and providing concise explanations. Our code is available at https://github.com/Antonio-Dee/M-CBM.

AI.tldr: Mechanistic Concept Bottleneck Models (M-CBM) enhance interpretability by deriving concepts directly from black-box models instead of relying on predefined concepts, improving predictive power and explanation quality.
AI.motivation: Traditional Concept Bottleneck Models face limitations due to reliance on predefined concepts that may lack predictive power or be difficult to learn from data.
AI.method: M-CBM extracts concepts using Sparse Autoencoders and annotates them with a Multimodal LLM, offering a more adaptive bottleneck layer compared to predefined concepts.
AI.result: M-CBM achieves superior performance compared to prior CBMs, with improved concept predictions and explanations under controlled sparsity conditions.
AI.conclusion: M-CBMs provide a robust approach for interpretable models, achieving better results while adhering to sparsity constraints and enhancing explanation quality.

title: DogWeave: High-Fidelity 3D Canine Reconstruction from a Single Image via Normal Fusion and Conditional Inpainting
summary: Monocular 3D animal reconstruction is challenging due to complex articulation, self-occlusion, and fine-scale details such as fur. Existing methods often produce distorted geometry and inconsistent textures due to the lack of articulated 3D supervision and limited availability of back-view images in 2D datasets, which makes reconstructing unobserved regions particularly difficult. To address these limitations, we propose DogWeave, a model-based framework for reconstructing high-fidelity 3D canine models from a single RGB image. DogWeave improves geometry by refining a coarsely-initiated parametric mesh into a detailed SDF representation through multi-view normal field optimization using diffusion-enhanced normals. It then generates view-consistent textures through conditional partial inpainting guided by structure and style cues, enabling realistic reconstruction of unobserved regions. Using only about 7,000 dog images processed via our 2D pipeline for training, DogWeave produces complete, realistic 3D models and outperforms state-of-the-art single image to 3d reconstruction methods in both shape accuracy and texture realism for canines.

AI.tldr: DogWeave achieves detailed reconstruction of realistic 3D canine models from single images by refining geometry and improving texture consistency using advanced methods.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in monocular 3D animal reconstruction caused by articulation, occlusion, and limited back-view data.
AI.method: DogWeave refines coarse parametric meshes with detailed SDF representation and enhances textures through conditional partial inpainting guided by structure and style cues.
AI.result: DogWeave produces realistic and complete 3D canine models using only 7,000 training images, surpassing state-of-the-art methods in shape and texture accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a significant improvement in reconstructing high-fidelity 3D canine models from single RGB images.

title: EndoSERV: A Vision-based Endoluminal Robot Navigation System
summary: Robot-assisted endoluminal procedures are increasingly used for early cancer intervention. However, the intricate, narrow and tortuous pathways within the luminal anatomy pose substantial difficulties for robot navigation. Vision-based navigation offers a promising solution, but existing localization approaches are error-prone due to tissue deformation, in vivo artifacts and a lack of distinctive landmarks for consistent localization. This paper presents a novel EndoSERV localization method to address these challenges. It includes two main parts, \textit{i.e.}, \textbf{SE}gment-to-structure and \textbf{R}eal-to-\textbf{V}irtual mapping, and hence the name. For long-range and complex luminal structures, we divide them into smaller sub-segments and estimate the odometry independently. To cater for label insufficiency, an efficient transfer technique maps real image features to the virtual domain to use virtual pose ground truth. The training phases of EndoSERV include an offline pretraining to extract texture-agnostic features, and an online phase that adapts to real-world conditions. Extensive experiments based on both public and clinical datasets have been performed to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method even without any real pose labels.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces EndoSERV, a localization method for robot-assisted endoluminal procedures, addressing navigation difficulties caused by narrow and complex pathways.
AI.motivation: Current challenges with vision-based navigation include errors from tissue deformation, artifacts, and lack of distinct landmarks.
AI.method: EndoSERV divides complex luminal structures into smaller sub-segments for independent odometry estimation and employs a real-to-virtual mapping for training using virtual pose ground truth.
AI.result: Experiments on public and clinical datasets show EndoSERV's robustness in navigation, even without real pose labels.
AI.conclusion: EndoSERV provides an effective localization solution for challenging anatomy, enhancing accuracy in endoluminal robot navigation.

title: Learning Clinical Representations Under Systematic Distribution Shift
summary: Clinical machine learning models are increasingly trained using large scale, multimodal foundation paradigms, yet deployment environments often differ systematically from the data generating settings used during training. Such shifts arise from heterogeneous measurement policies, documentation practices, and institutional workflows, leading to representation entanglement between physiologic signal and practice specific artifacts. In this work, we propose a practice invariant representation learning framework for multimodal clinical prediction. We model clinical observations as arising from latent physiologic factors and environment dependent processes, and introduce an objective that jointly optimizes predictive performance while suppressing environment predictive information in the learned embedding. Concretely, we combine supervised risk minimization with adversarial environment regularization and invariant risk penalties across hospitals. Across multiple longitudinal EHR prediction tasks and cross institution evaluations, our method improves out of distribution AUROC by up to 2 to 3 points relative to masked pretraining and standard supervised baselines, while maintaining in distribution performance and improving calibration. These results demonstrate that explicitly accounting for systematic distribution shift during representation learning yields more robust and transferable clinical models, highlighting the importance of structural invariance alongside architectural scale in healthcare AI.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to address the problem of systematic distribution shifts in clinical machine learning by promoting invariant representation learning for multimodal data, improving robustness and transferability of clinical predictions.
AI.motivation: Clinical environments often differ from the settings where machine learning models are trained due to variations in measurement policies, documentation practices, and workflows. This leads to distribution shifts and representation entanglement, which can negatively impact model reliability and performance in deployment.
AI.method: The authors propose a practice invariant representation learning framework that models clinical observations as latent physiologic factors polarized by environment-specific variations. The method integrates supervised risk minimization with adversarial environment regularization and invariant risk penalties across different hospitals.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates an improvement in out-of-distribution AUROC by up to 2–3 points compared to baseline methods while preserving in-distribution performance and improving calibration on longitudinal EHR prediction tasks and cross-institution evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Addressing systematic distribution shifts explicitly during representation learning enhances the robustness and generalizability of clinical machine learning models, underscoring the need for structural invariance methods in healthcare AI.

title: Med-Evo: Test-time Self-evolution for Medical Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Medical Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse healthcare tasks. However, current post-training strategies, such as supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, heavily depend on substantial annotated data while overlooking the potential of unlabeled test data for model enhancement. This limitation becomes particularly pronounced in medical domains, where acquiring extensive labeled medical data is difficult due to the strict data sensitivity and annotation complexity. Moreover, leveraging test data poses challenges in generating reliable supervision signals from unlabeled samples and maintaining stable self-evolution. To address these limitations, we propose Med-Evo, the first self-evolution framework for medical MLLMs that utilizes label-free reinforcement learning to promote model performance without requiring additional labeled data. Our framework introduces two key innovations: $1)$ Feature-driven Pseudo Labeling (FPL) that identifies semantic centroids from all heterogeneous candidate responses to select pseudo labels in each rollout, and $2)$ Hard-Soft Reward (HSR) that combines exact match with token-level assessment and semantic similarity to provide hierarchical reward. Experiments on three medical VQA benchmarks and two base MLLMs show clear advantages of our approach over SOTA methods, with significant improvements of 10.43\% accuracy and 4.68\% recall on the SLAKE dataset using Qwen2.5-VL, showing the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: Med-Evo proposes a self-evolution framework for medical multimodal large language models that enhances performance without requiring labeled data through innovative pseudo-labeling and reward mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Acquiring labeled medical data is challenging due to data sensitivity, annotation complexity, and reliance on annotated data using current strategies.
AI.method: The paper introduces Feature-driven Pseudo Labeling (FPL) and Hard-Soft Reward (HSR) using label-free reinforcement learning to evolve models using unlabeled test data.
AI.result: Med-Evo achieves substantial accuracy and recall improvements on medical benchmarks compared to state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Med-Evo is a promising method to advance medical MLLMs by addressing labeled data limitations while achieving significant performance gains.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Modal Planning for Fixed-Altitude Sparse Target Search and Sampling
summary: Efficient monitoring of sparse benthic phenomena, such as coral colonies, presents a great challenge for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles. Traditional exhaustive coverage strategies are energy-inefficient, while recent adaptive sampling approaches rely on costly vertical maneuvers. To address these limitations, we propose HIMoS (Hierarchical Informative Multi-Modal Search), a fixed-altitude framework for sparse coral search-and-sample missions. The system integrates a heterogeneous sensor suite within a two-layer planning architecture. At the strategic level, a Global Planner optimizes topological routes to maximize potential discovery. At the tactical level, a receding-horizon Local Planner leverages differentiable belief propagation to generate kinematically feasible trajectories that balance acoustic substrate exploration, visual coral search, and close-range sampling. Validated in high-fidelity simulations derived from real-world coral reef benthic surveys, our approach demonstrates superior mission efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces HIMoS, a fixed-altitude framework to improve efficiency in searching and sampling sparse coral colonies via Autonomous Underwater Vehicles.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to overcome the inefficiencies in traditional exhaustive search strategies and the high-cost vertical maneuvers of adaptive sampling in monitoring sparse benthic phenomena such as coral colonies.
AI.method: The framework, HIMoS, uses a hierarchical two-layer planning architecture comprising a Global Planner for topological route optimization and a Local Planner for trajectory generation, utilizing heterogeneous sensor data.
AI.result: HIMoS, validated through high-fidelity simulations of coral reef surveys, achieves higher mission efficiency than current state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: HIMoS offers an energy-efficient and highly effective approach for sparse coral search-and-sample missions, proving its potential for advancing benthic phenomena monitoring.

title: Latent Generative Models with Tunable Complexity for Compressed Sensing and other Inverse Problems
summary: Generative models have emerged as powerful priors for solving inverse problems. These models typically represent a class of natural signals using a single fixed complexity or dimensionality. This can be limiting: depending on the problem, a fixed complexity may result in high representation error if too small, or overfitting to noise if too large. We develop tunable-complexity priors for diffusion models, normalizing flows, and variational autoencoders, leveraging nested dropout. Across tasks including compressed sensing, inpainting, denoising, and phase retrieval, we show empirically that tunable priors consistently achieve lower reconstruction errors than fixed-complexity baselines. In the linear denoising setting, we provide a theoretical analysis that explicitly characterizes how the optimal tuning parameter depends on noise and model structure. This work demonstrates the potential of tunable-complexity generative priors and motivates both the development of supporting theory and their application across a wide range of inverse problems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes tunable-complexity generative priors to improve performance in solving inverse problems, outperforming fixed-complexity models in various tasks.
AI.motivation: Typically generative models use a fixed complexity, which can result in errors due to underfitting or overfitting.
AI.method: The authors introduce tunable-complexity priors for models like diffusion models and variational autoencoders using nested dropout and provide empirical results and theoretical insights.
AI.result: Tunable priors achieved lower reconstruction errors in compressed sensing, inpainting, denoising, and phase retrieval tasks.
AI.conclusion: Tunable-complexity generative priors hold promise for optimizing inverse problem-solving and encourage further research and application in this domain.

title: Performance Comparison of IBN orchestration using LLM and SLMs
comment: Accepted for presentation at IEEE International Conference on Communications 2026
summary: The evolution of both 5G and 6G networks is driving the advancement of fully autonomous network management, placing Intent-Based Networking at the centre of this transformation. This paper introduces a novel framework for 5G and 6G IBN orchestration that leverages a stateful, hierarchical multi-agent architecture to achieve full automation using both SLMs and LLMs. Both models have been evaluated for translation accuracy using metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE-L, as well as computational complexity. Experimental results show that both models exhibit similar accuracy. However, result shows that SLMs can improve the overall completion speed of the IBN lifecycle by 20%.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an Intent-Based Networking (IBN) orchestration framework for 5G and 6G networks using hierarchical multi-agent architectures and evaluates its effectiveness with SLMs and LLMs.
AI.motivation: The evolution of 5G and 6G networks necessitates fully autonomous network management, with Intent-Based Networking playing a central role.
AI.method: The authors developed a hierarchical multi-agent architecture leveraging SLMs and LLMs, evaluated using translation accuracy metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE-L, along with computational complexity assessments.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that SLMs and LLMs have similar translation accuracy; however, SLMs improve the lifecycle completion speed of IBN orchestration by 20%.
AI.conclusion: SLMs are more efficient for completing the IBN lifecycle in 5G and 6G networks, suggesting their suitability for enhancing autonomous network management.

title: SLNet: A Super-Lightweight Geometry-Adaptive Network for 3D Point Cloud Recognition
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: We present SLNet, a lightweight backbone for 3D point cloud recognition designed to achieve strong performance without the computational cost of many recent attention, graph, and deep MLP based models. The model is built on two simple ideas: NAPE (Nonparametric Adaptive Point Embedding), which captures spatial structure using a combination of Gaussian RBF and cosine bases with input adaptive bandwidth and blending, and GMU (Geometric Modulation Unit), a per channel affine modulator that adds only 2D learnable parameters. These components are used within a four stage hierarchical encoder with FPS+kNN grouping, nonparametric normalization, and shared residual MLPs. In experiments, SLNet shows that a very small model can still remain highly competitive across several 3D recognition tasks. On ModelNet40, SLNet-S with 0.14M parameters and 0.31 GFLOPs achieves 93.64% overall accuracy, outperforming PointMLP-elite with 5x fewer parameters, while SLNet-M with 0.55M parameters and 1.22 GFLOPs reaches 93.92%, exceeding PointMLP with 24x fewer parameters. On ScanObjectNN, SLNet-M achieves 84.25% overall accuracy within 1.2 percentage points of PointMLP while using 28x fewer parameters. For large scale scene segmentation, SLNet-T extends the backbone with local Point Transformer attention and reaches 58.2% mIoU on S3DIS Area 5 with only 2.5M parameters, more than 17x fewer than Point Transformer V3. We also introduce NetScore+, which extends NetScore by incorporating latency and peak memory so that efficiency can be evaluated in a more deployment oriented way. Across multiple benchmarks and hardware settings, SLNet delivers a strong overall balance between accuracy and efficiency. Code is available at: https://github.com/m-saeid/SLNet.

AI.tldr: SLNet is a lightweight 3D point cloud recognition model with strong accuracy and efficiency, leveraging NAPE and GMU mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To provide a highly efficient model for 3D point cloud recognition without relying on computationally expensive attention, graph, or deep MLP-based methods.
AI.method: SLNet employs NAPE for spatial structure capturing, GMU for efficient modulation, four-stage hierarchical encoders, and introduces NetScore+ for deployment-oriented efficiency evaluation.
AI.result: SLNet demonstrates strong performance with significantly fewer parameters across 3D recognition tasks. On ModelNet40, SLNet-M achieves 93.92% accuracy; on ScanObjectNN, SLNet-M closely matches state-of-the-art performance with 28x fewer parameters; and for segmentation, SLNet-T outperforms competitors with high efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SLNet provides an optimal balance of accuracy and efficiency across multiple 3D recognition benchmarks, advancing deployment-friendly solutions for point cloud tasks.

title: PhaForce: Phase-Scheduled Visual-Force Policy Learning with Slow Planning and Fast Correction for Contact-Rich Manipulation
summary: Contact-rich manipulation requires not only vision-dominant task semantics but also closed-loop reactions to force/torque (F/T) transients. Yet, generative visuomotor policies are typically constrained to low-frequency updates due to inference latency and action chunking, underutilizing F/T for control-rate feedback. Furthermore, existing force-aware methods often inject force continuously and indiscriminately, lacking an explicit mechanism to schedule when / how much / where to apply force across different task phases. We propose PhaForce, a phase-scheduled visual--force policy that coordinates low-rate chunk-level planning and high-rate residual correction via a unified contact/phase schedule. PhaForce comprises (i) a contact-aware phase predictor (CAP) that estimates contact probability and phase belief, (ii) a Slow diffusion planner that performs dual-gated visual--force fusion with orthogonal residual injection to preserve vision semantics while conditioning on force, and (iii) a Fast corrector that applies control-rate phase-routed residuals in interpretable corrective subspaces for within-chunk micro-adjustments. Across multiple real-robot contact-rich tasks, PhaForce achieves an average success rate of 86% (+40 pp over baselines), while also substantially improving contact quality by regulating interaction forces and exhibiting robust adaptability to OOD geometric shifts.

AI.tldr: This paper presents PhaForce, a phase-scheduled visuomotor control system for contact-rich tasks that integrates visual and force-based feedback to significantly enhance manipulation capabilities.
AI.motivation: The limitation of current visuomotor policies lies in their underutilization of force/torque feedback due to their low-frequency updates, and a lack of explicit mechanisms to optimize force application across different phases of manipulation tasks.
AI.method: PhaForce introduces a unified contact/phase schedule consisting of three components: a contact-aware phase predictor (CAP), a Slow diffusion planner for dual-gated visual-force fusion, and a Fast corrector for micro-adjustments via phase-routed residuals.
AI.result: PhaForce achieved an average success rate of 86%, marking a 40 percentage point improvement over baselines, while also improving contact quality and exhibiting robust adaptability to geometric changes in manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: PhaForce demonstrates the effectiveness of integrating a phase-scheduled visual-force policy in enabling robust, adaptable, and efficient contact-rich manipulation.

title: N-Tree Diffusion for Long-Horizon Wildfire Risk Forecasting
summary: Long-horizon wildfire risk forecasting requires generating probabilistic spatial fields under sparse event supervision while maintaining computational efficiency across multiple prediction horizons. Extending diffusion models to multi-step forecasting typically repeats the denoising process independently for each horizon, leading to redundant computation. We introduce N-Tree Diffusion (NT-Diffusion), a hierarchical diffusion model designed for long-horizon wildfire risk forecasting. Fire occurrences are represented as continuous Fire Risk Maps (FRMs), which provide a smoothed spatial risk field suitable for probabilistic modeling. Instead of running separate diffusion trajectories for each predicted timestamp, NT-Diffusion shares early denoising stages and branches at later levels, allowing horizon-specific refinement while reducing redundant sampling. We evaluate the proposed framework on a newly collected real-world wildfire dataset constructed for long-horizon probabilistic prediction. Results indicate that NT-Diffusion achieves consistent accuracy improvements and reduced inference cost compared to baseline forecasting approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NT-Diffusion, a hierarchical diffusion model, aimed at efficient and accurate long-horizon wildfire risk forecasting using probabilistic modeling.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the inefficiency and computational challenges of long-horizon wildfire risk forecasting, especially due to redundant computations in traditional diffusion models.
AI.method: It proposes NT-Diffusion, which uses a hierarchical structure to share early-stage computations and performs horizon-specific refinements for forecasting wildfire risks represented as Fire Risk Maps (FRMs).
AI.result: NT-Diffusion outperforms baseline forecasting methods in terms of accuracy and reduces inference costs on a newly collected real-world wildfire dataset.
AI.conclusion: The model demonstrates that a hierarchical diffusion approach can effectively reduce computational redundancy and enhance the accuracy of long-horizon wildfire risk predictions.

title: 3ViewSense: Spatial and Mental Perspective Reasoning from Orthographic Views in Vision-Language Models
summary: Current Large Language Models have achieved Olympiad-level logic, yet Vision-Language Models paradoxically falter on elementary spatial tasks like block counting. This capability mismatch reveals a critical ``spatial intelligence gap,'' where models fail to construct coherent 3D mental representations from 2D observations. We uncover this gap via diagnostic analyses showing the bottleneck is a missing view-consistent spatial interface rather than insufficient visual features or weak reasoning. To bridge this, we introduce \textbf{3ViewSense}, a framework that grounds spatial reasoning in Orthographic Views. Drawing on engineering cognition, we propose a ``Simulate-and-Reason'' mechanism that decomposes complex scenes into canonical orthographic projections to resolve geometric ambiguities. By aligning egocentric perceptions with these allocentric references, our method facilitates explicit mental rotation and reconstruction. Empirical results on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baselines, with consistent gains on occlusion-heavy counting and view-consistent spatial reasoning. The framework also improves the stability and consistency of spatial descriptions, offering a scalable path toward stronger spatial intelligence in multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a spatial intelligence gap in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and introduces 3ViewSense to improve spatial reasoning using orthographic views, showing superior results in overcoming the gap.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the spatial intelligence gap in Vision-Language Models, where they fail to achieve basic spatial tasks despite significant logic capabilities, thus highlighting the need for a model to construct coherent 3D mental representations from 2D observations.
AI.method: The introduced method, 3ViewSense, utilizes orthographic views and a 'Simulate-and-Reason' mechanism to decompose complex scenes into canonical orthographic projections. This allows for explicit mental rotation, reconstruction, and alignment of egocentric and allocentric perceptions.
AI.result: The method significantly outperformed existing baselines in spatial reasoning benchmarks, particularly in occlusion-heavy counting and view-consistent spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: 3ViewSense enhances stability, consistency, and performance in spatial reasoning, offering a scalable solution to improve spatial intelligence in Vision-Language Models.

title: Perception-Aware Communication-Free Multi-UAV Coordination in the Wild
summary: We present a communication-free method for safe multi-robot coordination in complex environments such as forests with dense canopy cover, where GNSS is unavailable. Our approach relies on an onboard anisotropic 3D LiDAR sensor used for SLAM as well as for detecting obstacles and neighboring robots. We develop a novel perception-aware 3D navigation framework that enables robots to safely and effectively progress toward a goal region despite limited sensor field-of-view. The approach is evaluated through extensive simulations across diverse scenarios and validated in real-world field experiments, demonstrating its scalability, robustness, and reliability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a communication-free method for safe multi-robot coordination using 3D LiDAR navigation in environments where GNSS is unavailable.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in multi-robot coordination in complex environments like forests with dense canopy cover, where GNSS signals cannot be relied upon.
AI.method: Developing a perception-aware 3D navigation framework based on onboard 3D LiDAR sensors for SLAM, obstacle detection, and neighboring robot recognition.
AI.result: The method shows scalability, robustness, and reliability through extensive simulations in diverse scenarios and real-world field experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework ensures safe and effective multi-robot coordination even in GNSS-deprived, complex environments.

title: Scaling Laws in the Tiny Regime: How Small Models Change Their Mistakes
comment: 17 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to MDPI Machine Learning and Knowledge Extraction (MAKE)
summary:
Neural scaling laws describe how model performance improves as a power law with size, but existing work focuses on models above 100M parameters. The sub-20M regime -- where TinyML and edge AI operate -- remains unexamined. We train 90 models (22K--19.8M parameters) across two architectures (plain ConvNet, MobileNetV2) on CIFAR-100, varying width while holding depth and training fixed. Both follow approximate power laws in error rate: $α= 0.156 \pm 0.002$ (ScaleCNN) and $α= 0.106 \pm 0.001$ (MobileNetV2) across five seeds. Since prior work fit cross-entropy loss rather than error rate, direct exponent comparison is approximate; with that caveat, these are 1.4--2x steeper than $α\approx 0.076$ for large language models. The power law does not hold uniformly: local exponents decay with scale, and MobileNetV2 saturates at 19.8M parameters ($α_{\mathrm{local}} = 0.006$). Error structure also changes. Jaccard overlap between error sets of the smallest and largest ScaleCNN is only 0.35 (25 seed pairs, $\pm 0.004$) -- compression changes which inputs are misclassified, not merely how many. Small models
