AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of detecting fake review groups, especially in cold-start scenarios where traditional methods fail due to sparse data.
AI.method: The DS-DGA-GCN model emphasizes diverse and similarity-aware relationships within product-review-reviewer interactions. It incorporates a Network Feature Scoring (NFS) system and a dynamic graph attention mechanism for robust and adaptive detection.
AI.result: The model significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving detection accuracies of 89.8% on Amazon and 88.3% on Xiaohongshu datasets.
AI.conclusion: DS-DGA-GCN effectively detects fake reviewer groups with robust and adaptive capabilities, improving trust and fairness on online platforms.

title: Electrocardiogram Classification with Transformers Using Koopman and Wavelet Features
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis is vital for detecting cardiac abnormalities, yet robust automated classification is challenging due to the complexity and variability of physiological signals. In this work, we investigate transformer-based ECG classification using features derived from the Koopman operator and wavelet transforms. Two tasks are studied: (1) binary classification (Normal vs. Non-normal), and (2) four-class classification (Normal, Atrial Fibrillation, Ventricular Arrhythmia, Block). We use Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) to approximate the Koopman operator. Our results show that wavelet features excel in binary classification, while Koopman features, when paired with transformers, achieve superior performance in the four-class setting. A simple hybrid of Koopman and wavelet features does not improve accuracy. However, selecting an appropriate EDMD dictionary -- specifically a radial basis function dictionary with tuned parameters -- yields significant gains, surpassing the wavelet-only baseline and the hybrid wavelet-Koopman system. We also present a Koopman-based reconstruction analysis for interpretable insights into the learned dynamics and compare against a recurrent neural network baseline. Overall, our findings demonstrate the effectiveness of Koopman-based feature learning with transformers and highlight promising directions for integrating dynamical systems theory into time-series classification.

AI.tldr: The paper explores transformer-based ECG classification using Koopman operator and wavelet features, achieving state-of-the-art performance for specific tasks by leveraging dynamical systems theory.
AI.motivation: Automating ECG analysis is important for accurately identifying heart abnormalities, but signal complexity and variability make robust classification challenging.
AI.method: The study investigates binary and four-class ECG classification using transformers with Koopman operator-based features (using Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and wavelet transforms. Comparisons are drawn between these approaches and a recurrent neural network baseline.
AI.result: Wavelet features excel in binary classification tasks, but transformer models using well-tuned Koopman features outperform in the four-class classification scenario. A hybrid approach of Koopman and wavelet features does not yield improvements.
AI.conclusion: Koopman-based feature learning combined with transformers is effective for ECG classification, particularly in multi-class tasks, and offers interpretability. Dynamical systems theory shows promise for advancing time-series classification.

title: Scaling Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials with Mixtures of Experts
summary: Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) enable accurate large-scale atomistic simulations, yet improving their expressive capacity efficiently remains challenging. Here we systematically develop Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and Mixture-of-Linear-Experts (MoLE) architectures for MLIPs and analyze the effects of routing strategies and expert designs. We show that sparse activation combined with shared experts yields substantial performance gains, and that nonlinear MoE formulations outperform MoLE when shared experts are present, underscoring the importance of nonlinear expert specialization. Furthermore, element-wise routing consistently surpasses configuration-level routing, while global MoE routing often leads to numerical instability. The resulting element-wise MoE model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across the OMol25, OMat24, and OC20M benchmarks. Analysis of routing patterns reveals chemically interpretable expert specialization aligned with periodic-table trends, indicating that the model effectively captures element-specific chemical characteristics for precise interatomic modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper develops and assesses Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures for Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs), achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and demonstrating chemically interpretable expert specialization.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of efficiently improving the expressive capacity of Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) for precise large-scale atomistic simulations.
AI.method: The authors design and evaluate Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and Mixture-of-Linear-Experts (MoLE) architectures, testing various routing strategies and expert designs, focusing on sparse activation, nonlinear formulations, and element-specific routing.
AI.result: The proposed element-wise MoE model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on OMol25, OMat24, and OC20M benchmarks, with chemically interpretable routing patterns that align with periodic-table trends.
AI.conclusion: The presented approach enhances MLIP performance by employing nonlinear MoE architectures with element-specific routing, achieving precise modeling of interatomic interactions and expert specialization reflective of chemical properties.

title: Tau-BNO: Brain Neural Operator for Tau Transport Model
summary: Mechanistic modeling provides a biophysically grounded framework for studying the spread of pathological tau protein in tauopathies like Alzheimer's disease. Existing approaches typically model tau propagation as a diffusive process on the brain's structural connectome, reproducing macroscopic patterns but neglecting microscale cellular transport and reaction mechanisms. The Network Transport Model (NTM) was introduced to fill this gap, explaining how region-level progression of tau emerges from microscale biophysical processes. However, the NTM faces a common challenge for complex models defined by large systems of partial differential equations: the inability to perform parameter inference and mechanistic discovery due to high computational burden and slow model simulations. To overcome this barrier, we propose Tau-BNO, a Brain Neural Operator surrogate framework for rapidly approximating NTM dynamics that captures both intra-regional reaction kinetics and inter-regional network transport. Tau-BNO combines a function operator that encodes kinetic parameters with a query operator that preserves initial state information, while approximating anisotropic transport through a spectral kernel that retains directionality. Empirical evaluations demonstrate high predictive accuracy ($R^2\approx$ 0.98) across diverse biophysical regimes and an 89\% performance improvement over state-of-the-art sequence models like Transformers and Mamba, which lack inherent structural priors. By reducing simulation time from hours to seconds, we show that the surrogate model is capable of producing new insights and generating new hypotheses. This framework is readily extensible to a broader class of connectome-based biophysical models, showcasing the transformative value of deep learning surrogates to accelerate analysis of large-scale, computationally intensive dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Tau-BNO, a deep learning surrogate model that accelerates the simulation of tau protein propagation in tauopathies by approximating the computationally heavy Network Transport Model efficiently.
AI.motivation: To address the computational inefficiencies of the Network Transport Model in studying tau protein propagation, allowing for parameter inference and mechanistic discovery that are otherwise hindered.
AI.method: The proposed Tau-BNO framework combines a function operator for kinetic parameters, a query operator for initial state information, and a spectral kernel for anisotropic transport to approximate the dynamics of the Network Transport Model efficiently.
AI.result: Tau-BNO demonstrates high predictive accuracy ($R^2\approx$ 0.98), offers an 89% performance boost compared to sequence models like Transformers, and reduces simulation time from hours to seconds.
AI.conclusion: Tau-BNO not only accelerates simulation, but also enables new insights and hypothesis generation, representing a generalizable approach for other connectome-based biophysical models.

title: Outlier-robust Autocovariance Least Square Estimation via Iteratively Reweighted Least Square
summary: The autocovariance least squares (ALS) method is a computationally efficient approach for estimating noise covariances in Kalman filters without requiring specific noise models. However, conventional ALS and its variants rely on the classic least mean squares (LMS) criterion, making them highly sensitive to measurement outliers and prone to severe performance degradation. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes a novel outlier-robust ALS algorithm, termed ALS-IRLS, based on the iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS) framework. Specifically, the proposed approach introduces a two-tier robustification strategy. First, an innovation-level adaptive thresholding mechanism is employed to filter out heavily contaminated data. Second, the outlier-contaminated autocovariance is formulated using an $ε$-contamination model, where the standard LMS criterion is replaced by the Huber cost function. The IRLS method is then utilized to iteratively adjust data weights based on estimation deviations, effectively mitigating the influence of residual outliers. Comparative simulations demonstrate that ALS-IRLS reduces the root-mean-square error (RMSE) of noise covariance estimates by over two orders of magnitude compared to standard ALS. Furthermore, it significantly enhances downstream state estimation accuracy, outperforming existing outlier-robust Kalman filters and achieving performance nearly equivalent to the ideal Oracle lower bound in the presence of noisy and anomalous data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ALS-IRLS, an outlier-robust method for noise covariance estimation in Kalman filters, significantly improving accuracy and reducing sensitivity to outliers.
AI.motivation: Existing ALS methods for noise covariance estimation in Kalman filters suffer from high sensitivity to measurement outliers, causing performance degradation.
AI.method: The proposed ALS-IRLS algorithm employs a two-tier robustification strategy using innovation-level adaptive thresholding and the Huber cost function within an IRLS framework to mitigate outlier effects.
AI.result: ALS-IRLS significantly reduces estimation RMSE by over two orders of magnitude, enhances state estimation accuracy, and approaches the Oracle lower bound under noisy, anomalous conditions.
AI.conclusion: The ALS-IRLS method offers a robust and precise solution for noise covariance estimation, outperforming conventional and other robust Kalman filtering methods.

title: First-Order Geometry, Spectral Compression, and Structural Compatibility under Bounded Computation
summary: Optimization under structural constraints is typically analyzed through projection or penalty methods, obscuring the geometric mechanism by which constraints shape admissible dynamics. We propose an operator-theoretic formulation in which computational or feasibility limitations are encoded by self-adjoint operators defining locally reachable subspaces. In this setting, the optimal first-order improvement direction emerges as a pseudoinverse-weighted gradient, revealing how constraints induce a distorted ascent geometry. We further demonstrate that effective dynamics concentrate along dominant spectral modes, yielding a principled notion of spectral compression, and establish a compatibility principle that characterizes the existence of common admissible directions across multiple objectives. The resulting framework unifies gradient projection, spectral truncation, and multi-objective feasibility within a single geometric structure.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an operator-theoretic approach to optimization under structural constraints, revealing distorted ascent geometry caused by constraints, spectral compression, and unified principles for multiple-objective feasibility.
AI.motivation: To understand how structural constraints shape optimization dynamics beyond traditional projection or penalty methods.
AI.method: Formulating computational limitations as self-adjoint operators to define reachable subspaces and analyzing improvement directions using pseudoinverse-weighted gradients.
AI.result: Identified distorted ascent geometry due to constraints, concentration of dynamics along dominant spectral modes, and compatibility principles for multi-objective optimization.
AI.conclusion: Unified framework integrating gradient projection, spectral truncation, and multi-objective feasibility using geometric principles.

title: OSS-CRS: Liberating AIxCC Cyber Reasoning Systems for Real-World Open-Source Security
comment: Version 1.0 (March 2026), OSS-CRS: an open-source framework for porting, deploying, and composing AIxCC cyber reasoning systems. Project page: https://github.com/sslab-gatech/oss-crs
summary: DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) showed that cyber reasoning systems (CRSs) can go beyond vulnerability discovery to autonomously confirm and patch bugs: seven teams built such systems and open-sourced them after the competition. Yet all seven open-sourced CRSs remain largely unusable outside their original teams, each bound to the competition cloud infrastructure that no longer exists. We present OSS-CRS, an open, locally deployable framework for running and combining CRS techniques against real-world open-source projects, with budget-aware resource management. We ported the first-place system (Atlantis) and discovered 10 previously unknown bugs (three of high severity) across 8 OSS-Fuzz projects. OSS-CRS is publicly available.

AI.tldr: OSS-CRS is an open framework enabling the deployment and enhancement of cyber reasoning systems on open-source projects, porting the DARPA AIxCC's top system and discovering new bugs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to make open-sourced cyber reasoning systems from DARPA's AIxCC usable by broader audiences and applicable to real-world scenarios, addressing the issue of these systems being tied to defunct competition infrastructure.
AI.method: The researchers developed OSS-CRS, a locally deployable framework, which includes budget-aware resource management and integrates CRS techniques. They also ported the AIxCC-winning system Atlantis to the new framework.
AI.result: The framework allowed for testing on open-source projects, discovering 10 previously unknown bugs, including three of high severity, demonstrating its efficacy.
AI.conclusion: OSS-CRS bridges the gap in usability and application for open-sourced CRSs, offering a practical tool for advancing cybersecurity efforts in open-source software projects.

title: Sign Identifiability of Causal Effects in Stationary Stochastic Dynamical Systems
summary: We study identifiability in continuous-time linear stationary stochastic differential equations with known causal structure. Unlike existing approaches, we relax the assumption of a known diffusion matrix, thereby respecting the model's intrinsic scale invariance. Rather than recovering drift coefficients themselves, we introduce edge-sign identifiability: for a given causal structure, we ask whether the sign of a given drift entry is uniquely determined across all observational covariance matrices induced by parametrizations compatible with that structure. Under a notion of faithfulness, we derive criteria for characterising identifiability, non-identifiability, and partial identifiability for general graphs. Applying our criteria to specific causal structures, both analogous to classical causal settings (e.g., instrumental variables) and novel cyclic settings, we determine their edge-sign identifiability and, in some cases, obtain explicit expressions for the sign of a target edge in terms of the observational covariance matrix.

AI.tldr: This paper explores identifiability in continuous-time linear stationary stochastic models and relaxes prior assumptions about the diffusion matrix. It introduces edge-sign identifiability and develops criteria to evaluate it.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to expand identifiability analysis in stochastic differential equations by removing the restrictive assumption of knowing the diffusion matrix, allowing for more realistic and generalized models.
AI.method: The paper introduces 'edge-sign identifiability' and provides criteria for evaluating identifiability under faithfulness assumptions. These criteria are applied to both classical and novel causal graph structures.
AI.result: The study successfully derives identifiability criteria, and for specific causal structures, determines conditions for edge-sign identifiability alongside explicit expressions for certain cases.
AI.conclusion: This work enhances identifiability research in causal inference by accommodating intrinsic scale invariance and offers theoretical tools to assess and determine edge sign information in continuous-time stochastic models.

title: Leaderboard Incentives: Model Rankings under Strategic Post-Training
summary: Influential benchmarks incentivize competing model developers to strategically allocate post-training resources toward improvements on the leaderboard, a phenomenon dubbed benchmaxxing or training on the test task. In this work, we initiate a principled study of the incentive structure that benchmarks induce. We model benchmarking as a Stackelberg game between a benchmark designer who chooses an evaluation protocol and multiple model developers who compete simultaneously in a subgame given by the designer's choice. Each competitor has a model of unknown latent quality and can inflate its observed score by allocating resources to benchmark-specific improvements. First, we prove that current benchmarks induce games for which no Nash equilibrium between model developers exists. This result suggests one explanation for why current practice leads to misaligned incentives, prompting model developers to strategize in opaque ways. However, we prove that under mild conditions, a recently proposed evaluation protocol, called tune-before-test, induces a benchmark with a unique Nash equilibrium that ranks models by latent quality. This positive result demonstrates that benchmarks need not set bad incentives, even if current evaluations do.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how benchmarks can prompt developers to prioritize improvements for ranking, often misaligning incentives. It demonstrates that current benchmarks might lack equilibrium, but proposes a solution via the "tune-before-test" protocol for aligning incentives.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the misaligned incentives in competitive benchmarking, where developers optimize for leaderboard ranking rather than genuine model quality.
AI.method: The work models benchmarking as a Stackelberg game, analyzing the interactions between benchmark designers and competitive model developers, and explores both current practices and a proposed protocol.
AI.result: It is shown that standard benchmarks lack equilibrium between developers, leading to opaque strategy optimization, while the tune-before-test protocol induces a unique Nash equilibrium ranking models by their latent quality.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights flaws in current evaluation practices and demonstrates that better-designed benchmarks, like tune-before-test, can realign incentives and rank models accurately by quality.

title: Benchmarking Language Modeling for Lossless Compression of Full-Fidelity Audio
comment: Submitted for review at Interspeech 2026, 7 pages, 5 figures
summary: Autoregressive "language" models (LMs) trained on raw waveforms can be repurposed for lossless audio compression, but prior work is limited to 8-bit audio, leaving open whether such approaches work for practical settings (16/24-bit) and can compete with existing codecs. We benchmark LM-based compression on full-fidelity audio across diverse domains (music, speech, bioacoustics), sampling rates (16kHz-48kHz), and bit depths (8, 16, 24-bit). Standard sample-level tokenization becomes intractable at higher bit depths due to vocabulary size (65K for 16-bit; 16.7M for 24-bit). We propose Trilobyte, a byte-level tokenization schema for full resolution audio, improving vocabulary scaling from $O(2^{b})$ to $O(1)$ and enabling the first tractable 24-bit LM-based lossless compression. While LMs consistently outperform FLAC and yield state-of-the-art compression at 8-bit and 16-bit, we observe that compression gains become more modest as bit depth increases beyond 8-bit.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates using autoregressive language models for lossless audio compression, introducing a more scalable byte-level tokenization schema for higher bit depths.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to explore whether autoregressive language models can effectively compress full-resolution audio (16/24-bit) and outperform existing codecs, addressing prior limitations to only 8-bit audio.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel byte-level tokenization schema called Trilobyte, which enables tractable tokenization for 16-bit and 24-bit audio by reducing vocabulary scaling complexity.
AI.result: The language models surpassed FLAC and achieved state-of-the-art compression for 8-bit and 16-bit audio. However, compression performance gains diminished at higher bit depths such as 24-bit.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that language models can be effectively applied to lossless audio compression even with full-resolution audio using an innovative tokenization approach, but challenges remain for high bit depths.

title: Integrating Lagrangian Neural Networks into the Dyna Framework for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) is sample-efficient but depends on the accuracy of the learned dynamics, which are often modeled using black-box methods that do not adhere to physical laws. Those methods tend to produce inaccurate predictions when presented with data that differ from the original training set. In this work, we employ Lagrangian neural networks (LNNs), which enforce an underlying Lagrangian structure to train the model within a Dyna-based MBRL framework. Furthermore, we train the LNN using stochastic gradient-based and state-estimation-based optimizers to learn the network's weights. The state-estimation-based method converges faster than the stochastic gradient-based method during neural network training. Simulation results are provided to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed LNN-based Dyna framework for MBRL.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Model-Based Reinforcement Learning framework using Lagrangian Neural Networks (LNNs) to enforce physical laws and improve sample-efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional model-based reinforcement learning struggles with inaccurate dynamics prediction due to black-box methods that ignore physical laws, leading to inefficiencies when processing unfamiliar data.
AI.method: The paper incorporates Lagrangian Neural Networks (LNNs) within a Dyna-based framework while using both stochastic gradient-based and state-estimation-based optimizers to train the model.
AI.result: Simulation experiments confirm that the state-estimation-based method converges faster in training than the stochastic gradient-based approach.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LNN-based Dyna framework effectively improves the sample efficiency and convergence speed, making it a reliable approach for model-based reinforcement learning.

title: Characterization and upgrade of a quantum graph neural network for charged particle tracking
summary: In the forthcoming years the LHC experiments are going to be upgraded to benefit from the substantial increase of the LHC instantaneous luminosity, which will lead to larger, denser events, and, consequently, greater complexity in reconstructing charged particle tracks, motivating frontier research in new technologies. Quantum machine learning models are being investigated as potential new approaches to high energy physics (HEP) tasks. We characterize and upgrade a quantum graph neural network (QGNN) architecture for charged particle track reconstruction on a simulated high luminosity dataset. The model operates on a set of event graphs, each built from the hits generated in tracking detector layers by particles produced in proton collisions, performing a classification of the possible hit connections between adjacent layers. In this approach the QGNN is designed as a hybrid architecture, interleaving classical feedforward networks with parametrized quantum circuits. We characterize the interplay between the classical and quantum components. We report on the principal upgrades to the original design, and present new evidence of improved training behavior, specifically in terms of convergence toward the final trained configuration.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on upgrading quantum graph neural network (QGNN) models to reconstruct particle tracks in high luminosity LHC experiments, showcasing improvements in training and model convergence.
AI.motivation: As LHC experiments will involve larger and more complex events due to increased instantaneous luminosity, reconstructing particle tracks becomes more challenging, motivating the exploration of quantum machine learning techniques.
AI.method: The study upgrades a hybrid QGNN combining classical neural networks and quantum circuits to classify hit connections in tracking detector layers using simulated event graphs.
AI.result: Improved training behavior and better model convergence were observed in the upgraded QGNN design for processing high luminosity simulated datasets.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid QGNN architecture shows enhanced performance for particle track reconstruction in upgraded LHC experiments, highlighting promising advancements in quantum machine learning for HEP tasks.
